title: Changelog
text: First stable release. package now follows semantic versioning; 1.0.0 line constitutes stable user-facing function set whose signatures change v2.0.0. hbsaems fits area-level Hierarchical Bayesian Small Area Estimation models following methodological tradition Rao Molina (2015) doi:10.1002/9781118735855, computational implementation adapted parameterisation prior-specification conventions brms package (Bürkner, 2017) doi:10.18637/jss.v080.i01 Stan back-end. package designed support principled Bayesian workflow advocated Gelman et al. (2020) doi:10.48550/arXiv.2011.01808. release consolidates changelog every development cycle since 0.1.2 maintenance release.

Old usage continues work one-time soft-deprecation warning. removed v2.0.0. Title: “Hierarchical Bayesian Small Area Estimation Models” → “Hierarchical Bayesian Area-Level Small Area Estimation Models” make scope explicit Function hbm_generic() renamed hbm_flex() (old name removed) exported functions now use SAE-idiomatic snake_case names Comprehensive conflict checks: refusing prior/stanvars pinned parameters clear, actionable error messages Default priors centralised: Intercept ~ student_t(4, 0, 10), b ~ student_t(4, 0, 2.5), phi ~ gamma(alpha, beta) (sampled) Test suite reorganised CRAN-safe unit tests (tests/testthat/) heavy integration tests (tests/testthat/dev-tests/, gated skip_on_cran())

what: hbsaems: Model Pendugaan Area Kecil Bayesian Hierarki Berbasis Area
title: hbsaems: Model Pendugaan Area Kecil Bayesian Hierarki Berbasis Area
text: 🌏 Read English

title: hbsaems: Model Pendugaan Area Kecil Bayesian Hierarki Berbasis Area
text: hbsaems adalah paket R untuk Pendugaan Area Kecil (Small Area Estimation, SAE) Bayesian Hierarki Berbasis Area (HBSAE). Landasan metodologinya mengikuti literatur SAE standar – terutama Rao dan Molina (2015) – sementara implementasi komputasinya diadaptasi dari konvensi parameterisasi dan spesifikasi prior pada paket brms (Bürkner, 2017), yang menggunakan back-end Stan. Paket ini dirancang untuk mendukung alur kerja Bayesian yang prinsipil (Gelman dkk., 2020) sehingga pemodelan SAE menjadi sistematis: prior predictive checks, diagnostik konvergensi MCMC, posterior predictive checks, leave-one-cross-validation, perbandingan dan rata-rata model Bayesian, analisis sensitivitas prior, serta benchmarking design-consistent semuanya menjadi bagian dari pipeline standar. Cakupan. Paket ini fokus pada model SAE berbasis area. Model SAE berbasis unit (mis. model komponen galat dari Battese, Harter & Fuller, 1988) bukan fokus paket ini.

title: hbsaems: Model Pendugaan Area Kecil Bayesian Hierarki Berbasis Area
text: Model SAE berbasis area: Fay-Herriot normal, lognormal-lognormal, beta logit-normal, dan binomial logit-normal – masing-masing diadaptasi dari Rao dan Molina (2015) ke parameterisasi brms. Penamaan argumen idiomatik SAE (v1.0.0): argumen dinamai sesuai konsep SAE (auxiliary, area_var, spatial_var, spatial_model, sampling_variance, n, deff). Presisi beta: ϕi=ni/deffi−1\phi_i = n_i / \mathrm{deff}_i - 1 disematkan sebagai offset. Lognormal: σi=ψi\sigma_i = \sqrt{\psi_i} disematkan sebagai offset (Fay-Herriot lognormal). Mekanisme fixed_params generik untuk pengguna lanjutan dan distribusi kustom. Pengaruh acak spasial: CAR (ICAR / proper / BYM2) dan SAR (lag / error). Distribusi brms kustom: Loglogistic dan Shifted Loglogistic bawaan, ditambah kerangka registrasi untuk family buatan pengguna. Data hilang: penghapusan listwise, imputasi ganda (mice), atau imputasi Bayesian gabungan (brms::mi()) dengan auto-pilih. Alur kerja Bayesian komprehensif: prior predictive checks, diagnostik konvergensi MCMC, posterior predictive checks, LOO-CV, perbandingan dan rata-rata model Bayesian, analisis sensitivitas prior melalui priorsense, benchmarking design-consistent. Prior shrinkage: Horseshoe dan R2D2 yang dapat dipilih melalui prior_type. Suku mulus non-linier: thin-plate spline dan Gaussian process. Aplikasi Shiny interaktif: GUI dwibahasa (Inggris/Indonesia) melalui run_sae_app(). Sepuluh vignette komprehensif yang mencakup setiap model dan topik lanjutan.

title: hbsaems: Model Pendugaan Area Kecil Bayesian Hierarki Berbasis Area
code: library(hbsaems)  # Muat data contoh data("data_fhnorm") str(data_fhnorm[, c("regency", "province", "y", "D", "x1", "x2")])  # Pas model Fay-Herriot Normal dasar dengan IID area effect model <- hbm(   formula     = bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   hb_sampling = "gaussian",   hb_link     = "identity",   re          = ~(1 | regency),   data        = data_fhnorm,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 2, seed = 1 )  # Periksa ringkasan model summary(model)

title: hbsaems: Model Pendugaan Area Kecil Bayesian Hierarki Berbasis Area
text: Sebagian besar pengguna mulai dengan wrapper. Naik ke hbm_flex() ketika butuh family kustom atau antarmuka fixed_params generik; naik ke hbm() ketika butuh kontrol brms penuh.
code: hbm()                       Universal: family brms apapun, kustomisasi penuh                                        ▲                                        |                       hbm_flex()                       Registry family + auxiliary + fixed_params                                        ▲                                        |        hbm_lnln() / hbm_betalogitnorm() / hbm_binlogitnorm()        Wrapper ramah-SAE: response, auxiliary, area_var, ...

title: hbsaems: Model Pendugaan Area Kecil Bayesian Hierarki Berbasis Area
code: model <- hbm(   formula        = bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),  # rumus brms   hb_sampling    = "gaussian",            # family likelihood   hb_link        = "identity",            # fungsi link   re             = ~(1 | regency),        # IID area RE (rumus)   spatial_var    = "province",            # kolom RE spasial   spatial_model  = "car",                 # "car" atau "sar"   M              = adjacency_matrix_car,  # matriks bobot spasial   data           = data_fhnorm )

title: hbsaems: Model Pendugaan Area Kecil Bayesian Hierarki Berbasis Area
text: Beta logit-normal untuk proporsi dalam (0,1)(0, 1): Binomial logit-normal untuk jumlah sukses dari percobaan: Lognormal-lognormal (varian Fay-Herriot) untuk respons positif yang miring ke kanan:
code: fit_beta <- hbm_betalogitnorm(   response  = "y",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   n         = "n",                # kolom ukuran sampel   deff      = "deff",             # kolom design effect   area_var  = "regency",          # area random effect   data      = data_betalogitnorm,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 2, seed = 1 ) fit_bin <- hbm_binlogitnorm(   response  = "y",   trials    = "n",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var  = "district",         # 100 kecamatan pada data ini   data      = data_binlogitnorm,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 2, seed = 1 ) fit_lnln <- hbm_lnln(   response     = "y_obs",   auxiliary    = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var     = "district",   sampling_variance = "psi_i",          # variansi sampling (skala log)   data         = data_lnln,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 2, seed = 1 )

title: hbsaems: Model Pendugaan Area Kecil Bayesian Hierarki Berbasis Area
text: hbsaems menyertakan dua family brms kustom untuk data positif yang miring ke kanan: Loglogistic – parameterisasi Fisk kanonik mengikuti flexsurv::dllogis dan Wikipedia. Lihat ?loglogistic dan ?brms_custom_loglogistic. Shifted Loglogistic – parameterisasi gaya GEV dari Hosking & Wallis (1997). Lihat ?shifted_loglogistic dan ?brms_custom_shifted_loglogistic. Keduanya diregistrasi otomatis ke brms saat paket dimuat melalui register_hbsae_brms_custom().

title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: fitting model, run check_data() verify variables exist, identify missing patterns, get recommendation handle_missing strategy. check_data() returns object class c("hbsaems_data_check", "hbsaems_check"), can test generic predicate:
code: data("data_fhnorm")  chk <- check_data(   data       = data_fhnorm,   response   = "y",   auxiliary  = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   spatial_var = "province" )  print(chk) #>  HBSAE Data Check  [hbsaems_data_check] #>  --------------------------------------- #>   Observations    : 50 #>   Missing pattern : none (data complete) #>   Issues          : none #> #>   - No missing values detected. handle_missing can stay NULL. is.hbsaems_check(chk)   # TRUE

what: When the response has missing values
title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: Missing values Y often correspond non-sample areas – domains want prediction direct estimate. check_data() highlights explicitly: rows really non-sample areas, keep : fit model complete-Y subset use sae_predict(..., newdata = na_rows).
code: d2 <- data_fhnorm d2$y[1:5] <- NA chk2 <- check_data(d2, response = "y", auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3")) chk2$non_sample_warning

title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: build_spatial_weight() constructs CAR-ready binary matrix SAR-ready row-standardised matrix polygon shapefile: Distance-band rook contiguity also supported – see ?build_spatial_weight.
code: # Queen contiguity + binary -> for CAR (Besag 1974) M_car <- build_spatial_weight(   shp       = "path/to/areas.shp",   for_model = "car"         # implies type = "queen", style = "B" )  # K-nearest + row-standardised -> for SAR (Anselin 1988) M_sar <- build_spatial_weight(   shp       = "path/to/areas.shp",   for_model = "sar",        # implies type = "knn", style = "W"   k         = 4 )

title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: check_spatial_weight() runs five checks requirements chosen model class: catches common mistakes: non-symmetric matrices fed CAR, isolated areas ICAR, etc. also runs automatically inside hbm() whenever spatial_model set.
code: check_spatial_weight(M_car, spatial_model = "car") #> Spatial Weight Matrix Diagnostic #> --------------------------------- #>   Square          : TRUE #>   Zero diagonal   : TRUE #>   Symmetric       : TRUE #>   Detected style  : B #>   Isolated areas  : 0 #>   Components      : 1 #>   Matrix is theoretically compatible.

title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: common SAE scenario: estimates per district (kecamatan Indonesian) need roll published regency-level (kabupaten Indonesian) total – example, BPS, Indonesian central statistics office. sae_benchmark() method = "raking" handles : adjustment factor constant within regency different across regencies – exactly multi-domain benchmarking requires.
code: # Suppose we have SAE estimates per district, with a regency column # tagging which regency each district belongs to. estimates <- sae_predict(fit_hbm)  # Official regency-level totals (e.g.\ from BPS publications) T_regency <- c(Bogor = 110, Sukabumi = 145, Cianjur = 145)  bm <- sae_benchmark(   predictions = estimates,   target      = T_regency,            # one target per regency   weights     = my_data$population,  # population per district   groups      = my_data$regency,      # mapping district -> regency   method      = "raking" )  bm$benchmark_info$converged    # raking with one group var: converges in 1 sweep

title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: critical rule: sum(weights * theta) == target, consistent units. Two common pitfalls:

title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: default sae_benchmark() mode applies adjustment posterior mean – SD column approximated unchanged. understates uncertainty multiplicative ratios - understates additive shifts. fully Bayesian mode applies benchmark every posterior draw recomputes SD, RSE, quantiles correctly:
code: # Pass posterior = TRUE if predictions object carries draws bm_bayes <- sae_benchmark(   predictions = estimates,   target      = 1000,   method      = "ratio",   posterior   = TRUE,                # NEW in v1.0.0   probs       = c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975) )  bm_bayes$result_table #>    Prediction      SD  RSE_percent      Q025      Q50      Q975 #> 1     ...           ...     ...          ...      ...      ...  # Or supply your own draws matrix (D x n).  Use posterior_epred() so the # draws represent the area mean theta_i -- the quantity the benchmark # formula below adjusts (theta_{d,i} * r_d), and consistent with # sae_predict()'s v1.1.0 epred default.  Use posterior_predict() instead # only when you are benchmarking new-observation/aggregate-count draws. draws <- posterior_epred(fit$model) bm_bayes2 <- sae_benchmark(estimates, target = 1000,                             method = "ratio",                             posterior = draws)

title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: multiplicative ratio benchmarking, adjustment factor random variable computed draws: rd=T/∑jwjθd,j,θd,iB=θd,⋅rd.   r_d = T \, / \, \sum_j w_j \, \theta_{d,j},   \qquad   \theta_{d,}^{B} = \theta_{d,} \cdot r_d.  Since θd,\theta_{d,} rdr_d negatively correlated (draw larger area-ii value implies smaller rdr_d), post-benchmark variance strictly smaller naive r2Var(θ)r^2 \, \mathrm{Var}(\theta) approximation. Fully Bayesian benchmarking captures exactly.

what: 5. Smooth terms: splines and Gaussian processes
title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: hbsaems exposes full brms canonical API smooth terms. Replace linear predictor smooth function:

previous_headings: 5. Smooth terms: splines and Gaussian processes
title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: Choosing basis (spline_bs):
code: # Default: thin-plate regression spline, basis dimension chosen by mgcv fit_spline <- hbm(   formula           = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   data              = data_fhnorm,   re                = ~ (1 | regency),       # area-level random effect (Fay-Herriot)   sampling_variance = "D",                    # known sampling variance (Fay-Herriot)   nonlinear         = c("x2", "x3"),   nonlinear_type    = "spline",   spline_k          = 7L,                    # basis dim (-1 = auto)   spline_bs         = "tp",                  # "tp" (default), "cr", "cs", "ps"   control           = list(adapt_delta = 0.99),   chains = 2, iter = 2000, refresh = 0 )

previous_headings: 5. Smooth terms: splines and Gaussian processes
title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: SAE applications ~100 areas, always use Hilbert-space approximate GP (HSGP) Riutort-Mayol et al. (2023): GP argument reference: Note: exact GP warning. gp_k = NA (default), exact GP fitted — scales O(n3)O(n^3). n>100n > 100, hbsaems emits immediate warning recommending HSGP basis size. Always set gp_k production SAE work.
code: # RECOMMENDED: HSGP with Matérn 5/2 covariance fit_gp <- hbm(   formula           = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2),   data              = data_fhnorm,   re                = ~ (1 | regency),   sampling_variance = "D",                  # known sampling variance (Fay-Herriot)   nonlinear         = "x1",   nonlinear_type    = "gp",   gp_k              = 20L,                  # basis dim — REQUIRED for n > 100   gp_cov            = "matern25",           # more stable than "exp_quad"   gp_c              = 1.25,                 # boundary scale (brms default)   control           = list(adapt_delta = 0.99),   chains = 2, iter = 2000, refresh = 0 )

previous_headings: 5. Smooth terms: splines and Gaussian processes
title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: wrappers (hbm_lnln, hbm_binlogitnorm, hbm_betalogitnorm) flexible factory hbm_flex() accept smooth-term arguments. use hbm_nonlinear() config helper:
code: # Build the smooth-term spec once, reuse across models nl <- hbm_nonlinear(c("x1"), type = "gp",                      k = 20, gp_cov = "matern25")  fit <- hbm_flex(family_key = "lognormal",                  response  = "y_obs",                  auxiliary = c("x1", "x2"),                  area_var  = "district",                  data      = data_lnln,                  nl)                       # spliced in automatically

what: 5.4 Convergence advice for smooth-term SAE models
title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: Hierarchical Bayesian SAE models smooth terms prone recurring issues. Run convergence_check() every fit; recommendations address common pathologies. 1. Divergent transitions (common). Cause usually “funnel” area-level standard deviation area intercepts. Fix: Set control = list(adapt_delta = 0.99, max_treedepth = 12). Tighten prior area SD: set_prior("normal(0, 0.5)", class = "sd"). 2. Low effective sample size (ESS < 1000): Increase iter 6000 warmup = 3000. Centre scale auxiliary variables fitting. Run prior_check() (sample_prior = “”) rule prior–data conflict. 3. Exact GP n>100n > 100 — always hits max_treedepth /diverges. Set gp_k. See §5.2 . 4. Spline collinearity. auxiliary variables correlated: Switch spline_bs = "cr" better numerical conditioning. Use spline_bs = "cs" want shrinkage-driven selection smooth terms. 5. Spatial random effects. See vignette("hbsaems-spatial") BYM2 recommendation.

title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: hbsaems exposes brms-canonical global-local shrinkage priors. horseshoe (Piironen & Vehtari 2017) R2D2 (Zhang et al. 2022) available, can combined splines GPs.

title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
code: fit_hs <- hbm(   formula        = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3 + x4 + x5),   data           = my_data,   re             = ~ (1 | regency),   prior_type     = "horseshoe",   hs_df          = 1,        # local half-t df  (1 = original HS)   hs_df_global   = 1,        # global half-t df   hs_df_slab     = 4,        # slab half-t df   (regularised HS+)   hs_scale_slab  = 2,        # slab scale   hs_autoscale   = TRUE,     # scale with residual sigma (Gaussian only)   chains = 2, iter = 2000, refresh = 0 )

title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
code: fit_r2d2 <- hbm(   formula        = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3 + x4 + x5),   data           = my_data,   re             = ~ (1 | regency),   prior_type     = "r2d2",   r2d2_mean_R2   = 0.5,      # prior mean of R^2   r2d2_prec_R2   = 2,        # prior precision of R^2   r2d2_autoscale = TRUE,   chains = 2, iter = 2000, refresh = 0 )

what: 6.3 Cascading shrinkage across smooth and GP terms
title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: model combines parametric coefficients non-parametric smooth terms (s()) Gaussian processes (gp()), principled approach shrink components jointly. hbsaems automatically using brms-canonical main = TRUE pattern: Internally hbsaems builds following brms prior structure: main = TRUE flag b prior tells brms apply horseshoe jointly coefficients smooth/GP standard deviations. Without cascade, spline wiggliness GP variance regularised (uninformative) default priors, undermining shrinkage parametric part. cascade works R2D2 combination s() + gp() terms:
code: # Model with linear coefficients + spline + Gaussian process fit_combined <- hbm(   formula        = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   data           = my_data,   re             = ~ (1 | regency),   nonlinear      = c("x2", "x3"),   nonlinear_type = "spline",       # spline for x2 AND x3   prior_type     = "horseshoe",    # cascades automatically   chains = 2, iter = 2000, refresh = 0 ) horseshoe(df = 1, df_global = 1, df_slab = 4, scale_slab = 2, main = TRUE)  # class "b" horseshoe(df = 1, df_global = 1, df_slab = 4, scale_slab = 2)               # class "sds"

title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: hs_autoscale / r2d2_autoscale flags control whether brms re-scales prior using residual standard deviation σ\sigma. right thing continuous responses (Gaussian, lognormal) σ\sigma undefined discrete families. Beta, binomial, Poisson responses, set autoscale = FALSE: priors work family registry (Gaussian, Beta, binomial, lognormal, loglogistic, custom…) combine splines / GPs without extra setup.
code: fit_bin_hs <- hbm_binlogitnorm(   response       = "y",   trials         = "n",   auxiliary      = c("x1", "x2", "x3", "x4", "x5"),   area_var       = "district",   data           = data_binlogitnorm,   prior_type     = "horseshoe",   hs_autoscale   = FALSE,            # binomial: no residual SD to scale by   chains = 2, iter = 2000, refresh = 0 )

title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: model registry (R/models-registry.R) catalogues every model spec hbsaems knows . spec bundles likelihood family, default link, optional auxiliary-parameter hyperpriors, missing-data support flags, response-domain checks. Registered models usable via hbm(), convenience wrappers, flexible factory hbm_flex().

title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
code: list_hbsae_models() #> [1] "Beta" "beta-binomial" "binomial" "categorical" ...  get_hbsae_model("beta") #> $family             : "Beta" #> $link               : "logit" #> $discrete           : FALSE #> $supports_mi        : TRUE #> $aux_param_hyperprior: NULL                          # phi uses brms default                                                        # gamma(0.01, 0.01) since v1.0.0

what: Adding a custom model (Tier-1: pure metadata)
title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: distributions just need name, link, domain check:
code: register_hbsae_model(   key                = "gamma_log",   family             = "Gamma",   link               = "log",   discrete           = FALSE,   supports_mi        = TRUE,   response_check     = function(y) all(y > 0, na.rm = TRUE),   response_check_msg = "Gamma response must be strictly positive." )  # Use immediately fit_gamma <- hbm_flex(   family_key = "gamma_log",   response   = "expenditure",   auxiliary  = c("age", "income", "education"),   area_var   = "kecamatan",                    # area-level random effect   data       = my_household_data )

what: Adding a custom model (Tier-2: auxiliary parameter hyperprior)
title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: distributions auxiliary parameters (e.g. ϕ\phi Beta, shape Gamma):
code: register_hbsae_model(   key            = "gamma_with_hyperprior",   family         = "Gamma",   link           = "log",   response_check = function(y) all(y > 0, na.rm = TRUE),    # Inject Stan code via stanvars + a prior on the shape parameter   aux_param_hyperprior = function(args, data) {     if (!isTRUE(args$add_shape_prior)) return(NULL)     list(       stanvars = brms::stanvar(                   scode = "real<lower=0> shape_a;\n  real<lower=0> shape_b;",                   block = "parameters") +                 brms::stanvar(                   scode = "shape_a ~ gamma(1, 1);\n  shape_b ~ gamma(1, 1);",                   block = "model"),       prior    = brms::set_prior("gamma(shape_a, shape_b)", class = "shape")     )   } )  # Trigger the hyperprior via aux_args fit <- hbm_flex(   family_key = "gamma_with_hyperprior",   response   = "y",   auxiliary  = c("x1", "x2"),   area_var   = "area_id",                      # area-level random effect   data       = my_data,   aux_args   = list(add_shape_prior = TRUE) )

what: 8. Bayesian model averaging with LOO weights
title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: several candidate models pass convergence posterior-predictive checks, model averaging lets hedge across candidates rather committing single best model. model_average() supports three weighting strategies: returned hbsae_results object can passed sae_benchmark() exactly like single-model estimate.
code: # Fit two competing models fit_linear <- hbm(brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),                    data = my_data, re = ~(1 | regency)) fit_smooth <- hbm(brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),                    data           = my_data,                    re             = ~(1 | regency),                    nonlinear      = c("x2", "x3"),                    nonlinear_type = "spline")  # Stacking (recommended Bayesian model averaging) avg_stack <- model_average(fit_linear, fit_smooth,                             method = "stacking")  # Inspect the computed weights attr(avg_stack, "weights") #> [1] 0.34 0.66 attr(avg_stack, "weight_method") #> [1] "stacking"

previous_headings: 8. Bayesian model averaging with LOO weights
title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: LOO weights – LOO/WAIC comparisons general – trustworthy PSIS approximation stable, .e. Pareto- diagnostics 0.7 (Vehtari, Gelman & Gabry 2017). v1.1.0, model_compare() model_compare_all() check automatically: model_compare_all() adds n_high_k column, warning emitted . high- points exist, refit just folds exactly rather relying importance-sampling approximation. Supplying reloo_args triggers brms::reloo() (previously argument inert): cheaper alternative full refit moment_match = TRUE, applies moment-matching correction existing draws; use reloo_args moment matching insufficient.
code: # Compare two models; if any Pareto-k > 0.7, refit those folds exactly. cmp <- model_compare(   fit_linear, fit_smooth,   reloo_args = list(chains = 2)   # forwarded to brms::reloo() )  # Multi-model ranking surfaces the high-k count per model: rank <- model_compare_all(linear = fit_linear, smooth = fit_smooth) rank[, c("Model", "ELPD_LOO", "n_high_k")]

title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: convergence verified, next question : . prior_sensitivity() wraps modern power-scaling diagnostics Kallioinen et al. (2024) detect: Prior-data conflict – posterior moves non-negligibly prior - -weighted. Often indicates overly informative misspecified prior. Weak likelihood – posterior dominated prior. Common SAE areas sampled units. non-“-” diagnosis flags parameter warrants closer inspection; hypothetical output, coefficient x2 shows prior-data conflict, suggesting horseshoe prior strong covariate. prior_sensitivity() requires package; install install.packages("priorsense"). realistic workflow combining everything:
code: fit <- hbm(brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),            data           = my_data,            re             = ~(1 | regency),            prior_type     = "horseshoe",            chains = 4, iter = 4000)  # Report power-scaling diagnostics for every monitored parameter ps <- prior_sensitivity(fit) print(ps) #> # A tibble: 8 x 4 #>   variable                  prior  likelihood diagnosis #>   <chr>                     <dbl>       <dbl> <chr> #> 1 b_Intercept              0.012        0.821 - #> 2 b_x1                     0.156        0.683 - #> 3 b_x2                     0.834        0.412 prior-data conflict #> ... # 1. Check data chk <- check_data(my_data, response = "y",                    auxiliary  = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),                    spatial_var = "kecamatan")  # 2. Build CAR matrix from shapefile M <- build_spatial_weight("kecamatan.shp",                             for_model = "car",                             id_col    = "kec_code")  # 3. Configure with helpers (v1.0.0+); pass bundles directly to hbm() priors <- hbm_priors(prior_type = "horseshoe", hs_df_slab = 4) nl     <- hbm_nonlinear(c("x1"), type = "spline", k = 5) ctrl   <- hbm_control(chains = 4, iter = 4000, cores = 4,                        adapt_delta = 0.95)  # 4. Fit fit <- hbm(   formula = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   data    = my_data,   spatial_var = "kecamatan", spatial_model = "car", M = M,   handle_missing = chk$recommended_method,   priors, nl, ctrl )  # 5. Predict at all areas (including non-sample) est <- sae_predict(fit)  # 6. Benchmark to regency totals (fully Bayesian) T_kab <- c(Bogor = 110, Sukabumi = 145, Cianjur = 145) bm <- sae_benchmark(est,                      target  = T_kab,                      weights = my_data$populasi,                      groups  = my_data$regency,                      method  = "raking",                      posterior = TRUE)  # 7. Use the corrected uncertainty bm$result_table

title: Advanced Features: Spatial, Benchmarking, Custom Families, Smooth Terms
text: Many SAE designs multiple geographic levels – e.g. regencies within provinces, districts within counties within province. hbsaems supports directly area_var argument, now accepts character vector column names highest level : Internally helper builds brms / lme4 formula (1 | province / regency), Stan expands two independent normal random intercepts: αj∼𝒩(0,σprov2)βij∼𝒩(0,σreg2),   \alpha_{j} \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{\text{prov}}^{2}) \qquad   \beta_{ij}  \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_{\text{reg}}^{2}) , linear predictor area ii province jj becomes 𝐱ij⊤𝛃+αj+βij\mathbf x_{ij}^\top \boldsymbol\beta + \alpha_{j} + \beta_{ij}. canonical multi-stage SAE specification (Rao & Molina 2015, Ch. 8). use "crossed" instead "nested"? Use crossed two grouping variables share levels across – e.g.urban / rural across provinces. Pass area_re_structure = "crossed": becomes (1 | province) + (1 | urbanrural). Three levels. syntax extends arbitrarily: produces (1 | province / regency / district / village) brms expands four nested random effects.
code: # Two-level hierarchy: regency nested within province fit <- hbm_lnln(   response          = "y_obs",   auxiliary         = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   data              = my_data,   area_var          = c("province", "regency"),   # province first!   area_re_structure = "nested",                   # default   sampling_variance = "psi_i" ) fit_crossed <- hbm_lnln(   response          = "y_obs",   auxiliary         = c("x1", "x2"),   data              = my_data,   area_var          = c("province", "urbanrural"),   area_re_structure = "crossed",                   # NOT nested   sampling_variance = "psi_i" ) hbm_lnln(   response  = "y",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2"),   data      = my_data,   area_var  = c("province", "regency", "district", "village"),   # area_re_structure = "nested"   # default   sampling_variance = "psi_i" )

what: Why does hbsaems need to rewrite formulas at all?
title: AST-based Formula Manipulation: How hbsaems Rewrites Your Formula Safely
text: Users write simple, declarative formulas: actual brms formula passed Stan often needs richer. instance, high-level wrapper needs construct, internally: hbsaems reading sugar arguments (nonlinear, measurement_error, area_var, sampling_variance, handle_missing, …) transforming formula’s AST.
code: fml <- y ~ x1 + x2 + x3 hbm(   formula           = bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   data              = data_fhnorm,   hb_sampling       = "gaussian",   area_var          = c("province", "regency"),   nonlinear         = "x2",   measurement_error = list(x3 = "se_x3"),   sampling_variance = "D" ) bf(   y ~ x1 + s(x2) + mi(x3, se_x3) + (1 | province/regency),   sigma ~ 0 + offset(.hbsaems_sigma_fixed) )

what: Naive regex vs. AST: a cautionary tale
title: AST-based Formula Manipulation: How hbsaems Rewrites Your Formula Safely
text: Suppose wanted “replace x2 s(x2)” using regular expression: works simplest case. now try: output y ~ x1 + s(x2) + s(x2)1 + (s(x2)/100) broken three ways: x21 corrupted s(x2)1 (substring collision) (x2/100) rewritten even though user explicitly wrapped semantics () destroyed process naive regex solve three , require understanding structure formula – characters belong variable, subexpression wrapped function call, operator precedence places implicit grouping. understanding exactly AST (abstract syntax tree) gives .

title: AST-based Formula Manipulation: How hbsaems Rewrites Your Formula Safely
text: formula R string – call object, .e. node language tree: length(fml) == 3 formula ~ binary operator LHS RHS. can walk tree: RHS call +, call also length 3: Visually: principle write formula rewriter manual recursive walk tree. practice lean stats::terms() – already implements walk us returns clean list top-level term labels.
code: fml <- y ~ x1 + x2 + x3  class(fml) #> [1] "formula" typeof(fml) #> [1] "language" length(fml) #> [1] 3 fml[[1]]           # the root operator #> `~` fml[[2]]           # the LHS #> y fml[[3]]           # the RHS #> x1 + x2 + x3 fml[[3]][[1]]      # operator #> `+` fml[[3]][[2]]      # left subtree:  x1 + x2 #> x1 + x2 fml[[3]][[3]]      # right operand: x3 #> x3 `~`                    /   \                   y     `+`           <- RHS root                        /   \                      `+`    x3        <- nested +                     /   \                   x1    x2

title: AST-based Formula Manipulation: How hbsaems Rewrites Your Formula Safely
text: terms() decomposes RHS top-level terms leaving term’s internal expression unchanged: Key insight: term label string form term’s expression. (x2/100) one token, three: later ask “term equal ‘x2’?”, comparison strings, collisions impossible. core trick powers hbsaems’s formula sugar.
code: tt <- terms(y ~ x1 + I(x2 / 100) + s(x3) + x4:x5,             keep.order = TRUE) attr(tt, "term.labels") #> [1] "x1"        "I(x2/100)" "s(x3)"     "x4:x5" attr(tt, "intercept") #> [1] 1

title: AST-based Formula Manipulation: How hbsaems Rewrites Your Formula Safely
text: helper .replace_nl_in_formula() (called set nonlinear =) rewrites bare variable names smooth terms. algorithm: Let us see end--end: Note x10 x11 corrupted – different terms, different string labels. user explicitly wrapped x2 () suppress operator interpretation; hbsaems respects choice double-rewrite . interaction x1:x2 preserved bare – brms reliably fit s(x1):x2, conservative choice leave user’s interaction intact. last case subtle. stats::terms() strips offset() separate attr(., "offset") rather including term.labels – meaning earlier versions hbsaems silently lost offsets rewrite. v1.0.0 fix re-extracts positions splices back .
code: INPUT:  fml, nonlinear (character), nonlinear_type ("spline" or "gp")  STEP 1: tt <- stats::terms(fml, keep.order = TRUE)         Extract:  term.labels, intercept, offset positions, response  STEP 2: For each tl in term.labels:           if tl is exactly one of the names in `nonlinear`:             replace tl with `s(tl)` or `gp(tl, ...)`           else:             keep tl unchanged  STEP 3: Splice the offset() terms back in (terms() strips them into         a separate attribute).  STEP 4: Reassemble via as.formula(paste(..., "~", ...)) preserving env. # Round 1: simple hbsaems:::.replace_nl_in_formula(   fml            = y ~ x1 + x2 + x3,   nonlinear      = "x2",   nonlinear_type = "spline" ) #> y ~ x1 + s(x2) + x3 # Round 2: substring-name safety -- x1 is a prefix of x10 and x11 hbsaems:::.replace_nl_in_formula(   fml            = y ~ x1 + x10 + x11,   nonlinear      = "x1",   nonlinear_type = "spline" ) #> y ~ s(x1) + x10 + x11 # Round 3: wrapped variables are left alone hbsaems:::.replace_nl_in_formula(   fml            = y ~ x1 + I(x2/100) + x3,   nonlinear      = "x2",   nonlinear_type = "spline" ) #> y ~ x1 + I(x2/100) + x3 # Round 4: interactions left alone hbsaems:::.replace_nl_in_formula(   fml            = y ~ x1 + x1:x2 + x3,   nonlinear      = "x1",   nonlinear_type = "spline" ) #> y ~ s(x1) + x1:x2 + x3 # Round 5: GP (Gaussian process) instead of spline hbsaems:::.replace_nl_in_formula(   fml            = y ~ x1 + x2 + x3,   nonlinear      = "x2",   nonlinear_type = "gp",   gp_k           = 20,   gp_cov         = "exp_quad" ) #> y ~ x1 + gp(x2, k = 20, c = 1.25) + x3 # Round 6: offset() preserved across the rewrite hbsaems:::.replace_nl_in_formula(   fml            = y ~ x1 + offset(log(pop)) + x2,   nonlinear      = "x1",   nonlinear_type = "spline" ) #> y ~ s(x1) + x2 + offset(log(pop))

title: AST-based Formula Manipulation: How hbsaems Rewrites Your Formula Safely
text: multivariate brms-flavoured formulas, LHS mini-language addition operators (|), distributional wrappers (mi(), (), trials(), cens()), constructors (cbind(), mvbind()). need recover response column names – , instance, n y | trials(n). implementation small AST walker: recursively peels | operator (keeping LEFT branch time, RIGHT branch holds addition-wrappers want discard), asks .vars() bare names remain.
code: hbsaems:::.extract_response_names(quote(y)) #> [1] "y" hbsaems:::.extract_response_names(quote(y | mi())) #> [1] "y" hbsaems:::.extract_response_names(quote(y | trials(n))) #> [1] "y" hbsaems:::.extract_response_names(quote(y | cens(c))) #> [1] "y" hbsaems:::.extract_response_names(quote(cbind(s, f) | trials(n))) #> [1] "s" "f" while (is.call(lhs) && identical(lhs[[1L]], as.name("|"))) {   lhs <- lhs[[2L]] } all.vars(lhs)

title: AST-based Formula Manipulation: How hbsaems Rewrites Your Formula Safely
text: pass area_var = c("province", "regency") hbm(), helper builds random-effect specification: formula update()-ed onto main formula brms, producing final fitted model:
code: hbsaems:::.build_area_re_formula("province") #> ~(1 | province) #> <environment: 0x55c35a0a29c8>  hbsaems:::.build_area_re_formula(   c("province", "regency"),   structure = "nested" ) #> ~(1 | province/regency) #> <environment: 0x55c35a0e4460>  hbsaems:::.build_area_re_formula(   c("province", "regency"),   structure = "crossed" ) #> ~(1 | province) + (1 | regency) #> <environment: 0x55c35a12eba8>  hbsaems:::.build_area_re_formula(   c("province", "regency", "district", "village"),   structure = "nested" ) #> ~(1 | province/regency/district/village) #> <environment: 0x55c35a177130> y ~ x1 + x2 + (1 | province/regency/district/village)

title: AST-based Formula Manipulation: How hbsaems Rewrites Your Formula Safely
text: Putting together, rewriter pipeline request like walks following internal sequence (sketched): every step offset survives, wrapped mi() survives, substring collisions impossible, original environment formula preserved. None guarantees hold regex-based rewriter.
code: hbm(   formula           = bf(y ~ x1 + offset(log(pop)) + x2 + x3),   data              = data_fhnorm,   area_var          = c("province", "regency"),   nonlinear         = "x2",   measurement_error = list(x3 = "se_x3") ) fml_main <- bf(y ~ x1 + offset(log(pop)) + x2 + x3)$formula  # 1. measurement-error sugar fml_main <- hbsaems:::.apply_measurement_error(   fml_main, list(x3 = "se_x3") )$formula #> y ~ x1 + mi(x3, se_x3) + x2 + offset(log(pop))  # 2. nonlinear sugar fml_main <- hbsaems:::.replace_nl_in_formula(   fml_main, "x2", "spline" ) #> y ~ x1 + mi(x3, se_x3) + s(x2) + offset(log(pop))  # 3. area random effects (built separately, then update()d in) re_part <- hbsaems:::.build_area_re_formula(   c("province", "regency"), "nested" ) #> ~(1 | province/regency)  # Final formula handed to brms: #> y ~ x1 + mi(x3, se_x3) + s(x2) + offset(log(pop)) + #>     (1 | province/regency)

title: AST-based Formula Manipulation: How hbsaems Rewrites Your Formula Safely
text: can mix syntaxes freely. already know brms idioms, write directly formula – (x, se_x), poly(x, 2), (log(x)), offset(...), (1 | g), s(x), etc. hbsaems leave alone add sugar asked . surprising substring corruption. variable called x10 never confused x1, even x1 rewritten. invisible isn’t – failure mode naive rewriter silent corruption, error. Predictable composition. Sugar arguments compose: passing nonlinear measurement_error call produces formula expect, pass operates independent AST tokens. Future-proof. brms adds new addition operators smooth-term constructors, AST approach generally continues work without modification, treat top-level term opaque token.

what: Extending hbsaems: adding your own sugar
title: AST-based Formula Manipulation: How hbsaems Rewrites Your Formula Safely
text: contribute new sugar argument hbsaems, convention : template appears verbatim .replace_nl_in_formula() .apply_measurement_error() right pattern every top-level term rewrite.
code: .apply_my_sugar <- function(formula, my_arg) {   if (is.null(my_arg)) return(formula)    rewrite_one <- function(fml) {     tt <- tryCatch(stats::terms(fml, keep.order = TRUE),                     error = function(e) NULL)     if (is.null(tt)) return(fml)                # safer to skip than corrupt      term_labels   <- attr(tt, "term.labels")     has_intercept <- attr(tt, "intercept") == 1L     response_lang <- if (length(fml) == 3L) fml[[2L]] else NULL     env           <- environment(fml)      # Preserve offset() (terms() strips it)     offset_idx <- attr(tt, "offset")     offset_strs <- if (length(offset_idx)) {       vars <- attr(tt, "variables")       vapply(offset_idx, function(i)         deparse(vars[[i + 1L]], width.cutoff = 500L),         character(1L))     } else character(0L)      # YOUR REWRITE RULE per term label     new_labels <- vapply(term_labels, function(tl) {       # ... transform tl if it matches your criterion ...       tl     }, character(1L))      all_labels <- c(new_labels, offset_strs)     rhs_str <- if (length(all_labels) == 0L) {       if (has_intercept) "1" else "0"     } else {       paste(all_labels, collapse = " + ")     }     if (!has_intercept) rhs_str <- paste(rhs_str, "- 1")      if (!is.null(response_lang)) {       stats::as.formula(         paste(deparse(response_lang, width.cutoff = 500L), "~", rhs_str),         env = env       )     } else {       stats::as.formula(paste("~", rhs_str), env = env)     }   }    # Dispatch by formula class -- bf(), bf() + bf(), or plain formula   if (inherits(formula, "brmsformula")) {     formula$formula <- rewrite_one(formula$formula)     return(formula)   }   if (inherits(formula, "mvbrmsformula")) {     formula$forms <- lapply(formula$forms, function(f) {       f$formula <- rewrite_one(f$formula)       f     })     return(formula)   }   rewrite_one(formula) }

title: AST-based Formula Manipulation: How hbsaems Rewrites Your Formula Safely
text: take-away R’s language objects first-class data. Treating – rather strings sliced glued – buys correctness free cases string-based rewriter silently get wrong.

title: Complete Workflow with hbsaems 1.1.0
text: data_fhnorm simulated Fay-Herriot dataset: 100 regencies nested within 5 provinces, known sampling variance D per area three covariates.

title: Complete Workflow with hbsaems 1.1.0
text: sampling_variance = "D" argument defining feature Fay-Herriot model: sampling variance treated known design (estimated), brms pins observation-level . Omitting argument makes model unidentified residual area random-effect compete explain variance, typically producing divergent transitions wide credible intervals.
code: # This is the production call.  Replace `chains`, `iter` with your # usual values; the lighter settings below are a quick demonstration. model_prior <- hbm(   formula           = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   data              = data_fhnorm,   re                = ~ (1 | regency),     # area-level random effect (Fay-Herriot)   sampling_variance = "D",                 # KNOWN sampling variance D_i   sample_prior      = "only",   prior             = c(     brms::prior(normal(0, 1),  class = "b"),     brms::prior(normal(10, 5), class = "Intercept"),     brms::prior(normal(0, 2),  class = "sd")   ),   chains = 2, iter = 1000 ) pc <- prior_check(model_prior,                   data         = data_fhnorm,                   response_var = "y") plot(pc)

title: Complete Workflow with hbsaems 1.1.0
text: Two settings deserve attention: sampling_variance = "D" – column D data_fhnorm holds known sampling variance area; hbsaems translates offset observation-level standard deviation brms / Stan never tries estimate . adapt_delta = 0.99 – Fay-Herriot likelihoods can produce mild funnel geometry area random effects many areas small . v1.1.0 hbsaems already raises default brms’s 0.8 0.95; pushing 0.99 buys even conservative leapfrog step eliminates occasional divergent transition without re-parameterising model.
code: model <- hbm(   formula           = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   data              = data_fhnorm,   re                = ~ (1 | regency),   sampling_variance = "D",   control           = list(adapt_delta = 0.99),   chains            = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4 ) summary(model)

what: A small fitted model to demonstrate the rest of the workflow
title: Complete Workflow with hbsaems 1.1.0

code: model2 <- hbm(brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2), data = data_fhnorm,               re = ~ (1 | regency),               sampling_variance = "D",               control = list(adapt_delta = 0.99),               chains = 4, iter = 4000) model3 <- hbm(brms::bf(y ~ x1),      data = data_fhnorm,               re = ~ (1 | regency),               sampling_variance = "D",               control = list(adapt_delta = 0.99),               chains = 4, iter = 4000)  model_compare(model, model2) model_compare_all(full = model, medium = model2, simple = model3)

title: Complete Workflow with hbsaems 1.1.0
text: model averaging, fit several candidate models, either let model_average() weight stacking weights (LOO-based) supply weights:
code: # Stacking weights (default, derived from LOO) avg_stacked <- model_average(model, model2, model3)  # User-specified weights -- must sum to 1 avg_manual  <- model_average(model, model2, weights = c(0.7, 0.3))  # The returned object is an `hbsae_results`, identical in shape to # what sae_predict() returns, so all post-processing helpers # (sae_transform, sae_scale, sae_filter, sae_aggregate) work on it. plot(avg_stacked, type = "predictions")
