what: When to use the Beta likelihood for SAE
title: Beta Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: Many SAE problems involve response proportion, strictly 0 1: literacy rate, vaccine coverage, poverty headcount ratio, employment--population ratio. Gaussian linear model raw response conceptually wrong (can predict values outside [0,1][0,1]) Gaussian model logit-transformed response can poor true proportion near 0 1. Beta logit-normal model standard alternative (Liu 2009; Rao & Molina 2015, p. 390): yi∣θi,ϕi∼Beta(θiϕi,(1−θi)ϕi),logit(θi)=xi⊤𝛃+ui,ui∼𝒩(0,σu2), \begin{aligned} y_i \mid \theta_i, \phi_i    &\sim \mathrm{Beta}\!\bigl(\theta_i \phi_i, \, (1 - \theta_i)\phi_i\bigr), \\ \mathrm{logit}(\theta_i)    &= x_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta} + u_i, \quad      u_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_u^2), \end{aligned} θi∈(0,1)\theta_i \(0,1) area mean (parameterised logit scale) ϕi>0\phi_i > 0 precision parameter controlling within-area dispersion. larger ϕi\phi_i means less spread. survey-design data, ϕi\phi_i information-theoretic interpretation: ϕi=nideffi−1, \phi_i = \frac{n_i}{\mathrm{deff}_i} - 1, precision large effective sample size ni/deffin_i / \mathrm{deff}_i large. hbsaems lets either pin ϕi\phi_i value sample hyperprior.

title: Beta Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: hbm_betalogitnorm() recognises two distinct workflows: third path using generic fixed_params interface also available power users (see last section). Migration earlier versions. v1.0.0, random-phi mode used hierarchical construction ϕ∼Gamma(α,β)\phi \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta) hyperpriors α∼Gamma(1,1)\alpha \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(1, 1) β∼Gamma(1,1)\beta \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(1, 1). replaced brms’s default prior () Gamma(1,1)\mathrm{Gamma}(1,1) prior α\alpha (declared real<lower=1> Stan) boundary support routinely produced divergent transitions weakly-informative data; (ii) extra hyperprior layer inflated posterior dimension; (iii) new default plays cleanly prior = NULL meaning exactly “brms defaults”. need old construction, can build manually stanvars plus prior = set_prior("gamma(alpha, beta)", class = "phi") call – see ?hbm_betalogitnorm migration note.

title: Beta Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: bundled data_betalogitnorm typical SAE--survey structure: response y direct survey estimate area-level proportion; n deff come survey microdata; x1, x2, x3 area-level auxiliaries administrative census sources.

title: Beta Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: simplest call lets ϕ\phi sampled hyperprior: v1.0.0, ϕ\phi sampled brms’s default prior Gamma(0.01,0.01)\mathrm{Gamma}(0.01, 0.01) (lower bound 0) – weakly-informative choice prior mean 1 prior variance 100. Earlier hbsaems releases declared two extra Stan parameters alpha, beta hierarchical hyperprior; gone (see migration note ?hbm_betalogitnorm). Practical recommendation SAE applications. Real survey proportions typically yield ϕ∈[10,100]\phi \[10, 100]. default Gamma(0.01,0.01)\mathrm{Gamma}(0.01, 0.01) sufficiently uninformative data dominate posterior cases. posterior mean ϕ\phi wildly prior expectations, consider informed approach pinning ϕi\phi_i via Mode 2 , using survey-design information (nin_i design effect deffi\mathrm{deff}_i).
code: library(hbsaems) library(brms)  fit_random <- hbm_betalogitnorm(   response  = "y",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var   = "regency",   data      = data_betalogitnorm,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4,   seed = 1 ) summary(fit_random) ===== Hierarchical Bayesian Model Summary =====   Observations : 50  Family       : Beta (link: logit )  Formula      : y ~ x1 + x2 + x3 + (1 | regency)  ----- Parameter Estimates -----  Family: beta   Links: mu = logit; phi = identity Formula: y ~ x1 + x2 + x3 + (1 | regency)    Data: data_betalogitnorm (Number of observations: 50)   Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 4000; warmup = 2000; thin = 1;          total post-warmup draws = 8000  Multilevel Hyperparameters: ~group (Number of levels: 50)               Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS sd(Intercept)     0.21      0.06     0.10     0.33 1.00     1842     2615  Regression Coefficients:           Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS Intercept    -0.18      0.07    -0.31    -0.05 1.00     4527     5394 x1            0.36      0.04     0.28     0.43 1.00     6308     6541 x2           -0.21      0.04    -0.29    -0.13 1.00     5912     6105 x3            0.08      0.04     0.00     0.16 1.00     6184     6027  Further Distributional Parameters:        Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS phi      62.84     12.71    41.22    91.40 1.00     2841     3217  Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS).

what: Mode 2: ϕ\phi pinned from survey design
title: Beta Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: survey microdata available, pinning ϕi\phi_i known precision ni/deffi−1n_i / \mathrm{deff}_i - 1 informative typically gives smaller posterior intervals θi\theta_i: Observations: phi, alpha, beta lines Mode 1 gone – parameters longer sampled. CIs regression coefficients tighter (e.g. x1: [0.28,0.40][0.28, 0.40] vs [0.28,0.43][0.28, 0.43] Mode 1), since ϕ\phi uncertainty longer propagated.
code: fit_fixed <- hbm_betalogitnorm(   response  = "y",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   n         = "n",                   # <- column with sample sizes   deff      = "deff",                # <- column with design effects   area_var   = "regency",   data      = data_betalogitnorm,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4,   seed = 1 ) summary(fit_fixed) ===== Hierarchical Bayesian Model Summary =====   Observations : 50  Family       : Beta (link: logit )  Formula      : y ~ x1 + x2 + x3 + (1 | regency)                 phi ~ 0 + offset(.hbsaems_phi_fixed)  ----- Parameter Estimates -----  Family: beta   Links: mu = logit; phi = identity Formula: y ~ x1 + x2 + x3 + (1 | regency)    Data: data_betalogitnorm (Number of observations: 50)   Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 4000; warmup = 2000; thin = 1;          total post-warmup draws = 8000  Multilevel Hyperparameters: ~group (Number of levels: 50)               Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS sd(Intercept)     0.18      0.04     0.10     0.27 1.00     2843     3415  Regression Coefficients:           Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS Intercept    -0.16      0.05    -0.25    -0.07 1.00     5841     6024 x1            0.34      0.03     0.28     0.40 1.00     7158     6841 x2           -0.20      0.03    -0.26    -0.14 1.00     6915     6517 x3            0.09      0.03     0.03     0.15 1.00     7204     6624  Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS).

title: Beta Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: default brms prior ϕ∼Gamma(0.01,0.01)\phi \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(0.01, 0.01) suitable data, can override via prior argument: custom prior Gamma(2,0.05)\mathrm{Gamma}(2, 0.05) prior mean 2/0.05=402 / 0.05 = 40 prior variance 2/0.052=8002 / 0.05^2 = 800, informative choice SAE applications analyst expects ϕ\phi [10,100][10, 100] range. prior overrides class = "phi", default Intercept ~ student_t(4, 0, 10) b ~ student_t(4, 0, 2.5) still filled classes.
code: fit_phi <- hbm_betalogitnorm(   response  = "y",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var  = "regency",   data      = data_betalogitnorm,   prior     = brms::set_prior("gamma(2, 0.05)", class = "phi"),   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4,   seed = 1 ) Further Distributional Parameters:        Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS phi      54.91      9.83    37.65    76.20 1.00     3142     3674

title: Beta Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: Earlier hbsaems releases used ϕ∼Gamma(α,β)\phi \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta) α∼Gamma(1,1)\alpha \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(1, 1) β∼Gamma(1,1)\beta \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(1, 1). Starting v1.0.0, hbm_betalogitnorm() longer declares alpha beta Stan parameters, wrapper refuse legacy stanvar(..., scode = "alpha ~ gamma(1, 1);") pattern construction time informative error. Two reasons: hyperprior α∼Gamma(1,1)\alpha \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(1, 1) prior mean 11, boundary declared Stan parameter space (real<lower=1>). routinely produced divergent transitions weakly informative data. phi-prior plumbing inconsistent brms’s standard prior = interface, making hbm_betalogitnorm() harder teach harder compose brms features. new analyses use v1.0.0-supported pattern (non-default phi prior via prior =): specifically need reproduce pre-v1.0.0 ϕ∼Gamma(α,β)\phi \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta) construction (e.g. verify old analysis still gives answer), drop universal hbm_flex() interface, doesn’t v1.0.0 guard accepts arbitrary Stan parameters: Conflict policy. ϕ\phi pinned (Mode 2 Mode 4 ), longer Stan parameter – fixed model formula via offset. Supplying prior phi stanvars sampling statement targeting phi situation logical contradiction rejected clear error construction time. prevents silent surprises user thinks specified prior actually ignored.
code: fit_strong_phi <- hbm_betalogitnorm(   response  = "y",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var  = "regency",   data      = data_betalogitnorm,   prior     = brms::set_prior("gamma(2, 0.05)", class = "phi"),   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4,   seed = 1 ) # RECONSTRUCTION OF THE PRE-v1.0.0 HIERARCHICAL phi MODEL # # This is recommended only for replicating earlier results; for new # analyses prefer the prior-on-phi pattern above, which is much less # prone to divergent transitions. fit_legacy <- hbm_flex(   family_key = "beta",   response   = "y",   auxiliary  = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var   = "regency",   data       = data_betalogitnorm,   prior      = brms::set_prior("gamma(alpha, beta)", class = "phi"),   stanvars   =     brms::stanvar(scode = "  real<lower=1> alpha;", block = "parameters") +     brms::stanvar(scode = "  real<lower=0> beta;",  block = "parameters") +     brms::stanvar(scode = "  alpha ~ gamma(1, 1);", block = "model")     +     brms::stanvar(scode = "  beta  ~ gamma(1, 1);", block = "model"),   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4,   seed = 1 )

title: Beta Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: wrapper-specific n deff arguments syntactic sugar generic fixed_params mechanism works parameter family. Equivalent Mode 2: fixed_params argument accepts four kinds specifications – see ?hbm full table. pattern also works custom distributions registered via register_hbsae_brms_custom().
code: fit_flex <- hbm_flex(   family_key   = "Beta",   response     = "y",   auxiliary    = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var   = "regency",   data         = data_betalogitnorm,   fixed_params = list(phi = ~ I(n / deff - 1)),    # <- formula spec   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 1 )

title: Beta Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: every fit, standard diagnostic check : divergences appear, raise adapt_delta via control = list(adapt_delta = 0.99) consider tighter priors σu\sigma_u.
code: convergence_check(fit_fixed) $rhat_max [1] 1.0019  $ess_bulk_min [1] 2843  $ess_tail_min [1] 3415  $divergent_transitions [1] 0  $verdict [1] "All chains converged: Rhat < 1.01, ESS > 400, no divergent transitions."

title: Beta Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: survey microdata available, good practice compare Modes 1 2 see whether pinning ϕ\phi supported data: small elpd_diff (less 2 SE) suggests modes fit data comparably; pick simpler (fixed-ϕ\phi) model inference.
code: loo_compare <- loo::loo_compare(   loo::loo(fit_random$brmsfit),   loo::loo(fit_fixed$brmsfit) ) loo_compare elpd_diff se_diff fit_fixed         0.0       0.0 fit_random       -2.1       1.7

title: Beta Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: Predictions reported response scale (proportions (0,1)(0,1)), 95% credible intervals.
code: new_areas <- data.frame(   group = 51:55,   x1    = rnorm(5),   x2    = rnorm(5),   x3    = rnorm(5) ) preds <- sae_predict(fit_fixed, newdata = new_areas, allow_new_levels = TRUE) preds group estimate  lower  upper 1    51    0.421  0.298  0.561 2    52    0.508  0.379  0.643 3    53    0.347  0.231  0.482 4    54    0.612  0.481  0.731 5    55    0.385  0.262  0.524

what: Summary: which mode should I use?
title: Beta Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: Mode 1 (random ϕ\phi) – use n deff unknown unreliable; produces wider posterior intervals. Uses brms’s default prior Gamma(0.01,0.01)\mathrm{Gamma}(0.01, 0.01). Mode 2 (fixed ϕ\phi) – use clean survey design information; produces tighter intervals. Recommended standard SAE work. Mode 3 (custom prior ϕ\phi) – use strong prior beliefs typical magnitude ϕ\phi (e.g.Gamma(2,0.05)\mathrm{Gamma}(2, 0.05) survey-like data expected [10,100][10, 100]). Mode 4 (generic fixed_params) – use hbm_flex() building unusual models working custom distributions.

title: Beta Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: Liu, B. (2009). Hierarchical Bayes Estimation Empirical Best Prediction Small-Area Proportions. PhD thesis, University Maryland. Rao, J. N. K., & Molina, . (2015). Small Area Estimation, 2nd ed. Wiley. See Chapter 8 binary/Beta SAE.

what: When to use the binomial logit-normal model
title: Binomial Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: response count successes known number trials – say yiy_i vaccinated children nin_i surveyed area ii – binomial likelihood natural choice: yi∣pi,ni∼Binomial(ni,pi),logit(pi)=xi⊤𝛃+ui,ui∼𝒩(0,σu2). \begin{aligned} y_i \mid p_i, n_i &\sim \mathrm{Binomial}(n_i, p_i), \\ \mathrm{logit}(p_i)    &= x_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta} + u_i, \quad      u_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_u^2). \end{aligned} differs Beta model important way: trials nin_i exactly known (counted ) rather just used set precision parameter. Use model raw counts; use Beta model (hbm_betalogitnorm) direct proportion estimates known design effect.

title: Binomial Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: y raw success count, n trials, p = y / n direct estimate, x1, x2, x3 auxiliary variables.

title: Binomial Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: Family: binomial line y | trials(n) formula confirm wrapper correctly set binomial response passed trials column.
code: library(hbsaems) library(brms)  fit <- hbm_binlogitnorm(   response  = "y",   trials    = "n",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var   = "district",   data      = data_binlogitnorm,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4,   seed = 1 ) summary(fit) ===== Hierarchical Bayesian Model Summary =====   Observations : 100  Family       : binomial (link: logit )  Formula      : y | trials(n) ~ x1 + x2 + x3 + (1 | district)  ----- Parameter Estimates -----  Family: binomial   Links: mu = logit Formula: y | trials(n) ~ x1 + x2 + x3 + (1 | district)    Data: data_binlogitnorm (Number of observations: 100)   Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 4000; warmup = 2000; thin = 1;          total post-warmup draws = 8000  Multilevel Hyperparameters: ~group (Number of levels: 100)               Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS sd(Intercept)     0.24      0.05     0.14     0.34 1.00     1942     2734  Regression Coefficients:           Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS Intercept     0.08      0.04     0.00     0.16 1.00     5418     6107 x1            0.28      0.03     0.22     0.34 1.00     6802     6917 x2           -0.15      0.03    -0.21    -0.09 1.00     6517     6248 x3            0.07      0.03     0.01     0.13 1.00     6604     6321  Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS).

title: Binomial Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: spatially autocorrelated areas (e.g. neighbouring districts sharing characteristics), replace IID (1 | district) random effect CAR spatial random effect: new Correlation Structures: block reports spatial standard deviation sdcar. See vignette("hbsaems-spatial") SAR, BYM2, weight-matrix construction.
code: data("adjacency_matrix_car") fit_car <- hbm_binlogitnorm(   response  = "y",   trials    = "n",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   spatial_var = "regency",                       # spatial-RE column   spatial_model  = "car",                       # CAR structure   M         = adjacency_matrix_car,        # neighbour weight matrix   data      = data_binlogitnorm,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4,   seed = 1 ) summary(fit_car) Correlation Structures:       Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS sdcar     0.19      0.07     0.06     0.34 1.00     1843     2516  Regression Coefficients:           Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS Intercept     0.07      0.04     0.00     0.15 1.00     6018     6342 x1            0.26      0.03     0.20     0.32 1.00     7218     6917 x2           -0.14      0.03    -0.20    -0.08 1.00     6814     6539 x3            0.07      0.03     0.01     0.13 1.00     7104     6428

title: Binomial Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: impose tighter prior particular coefficient – example, constraining effect obviously non-informative auxiliary near-zero – supply brms::set_prior() object: Default priors (Intercept ~ student_t(4, 0, 10), b ~ student_t(4, 0, 2.5)) filled class don’t override.
code: fit_prior <- hbm_binlogitnorm(   response  = "y",   trials    = "n",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var   = "district",   data      = data_binlogitnorm,   prior     = brms::set_prior("normal(0, 0.1)", class = "b", coef = "x3"),   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4,   seed = 1 )

title: Binomial Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: y, n, auxiliary columns contain NA, wrapper auto-selects strategy unless supply handle_missing: Posterior samples five imputed fits pooled via Rubin’s rules returned hbmfit object. See vignette("hbsaems-handle-missing") full discussion.
code: data_with_na <- data_binlogitnorm data_with_na$x1[c(3, 14, 27)] <- NA          # 3 areas with missing x1  fit_miss <- hbm_binlogitnorm(   response  = "y",   trials    = "n",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var   = "district",   data      = data_with_na,   # handle_missing not given -> auto-select "multiple" (mice)   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4,   seed = 1 ) Missing values detected. Auto-selecting handle_missing = 'multiple' (family 'binomial', supports_mi = FALSE).  Missing predictor variable(s): x1. Applying multiple imputation (mice) with m = 5 imputations. Fitting imputed model 1... Fitting imputed model 2... ... Fitting imputed model 5...

title: Binomial Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: diagnostic prediction steps wrapper: Predictions unseen areas use marginal posterior random intercept.
code: convergence_check(fit)  new_areas <- data.frame(   group = 101:105,   n     = c(150, 120, 180, 110, 140),   x1    = rnorm(5),   x2    = rnorm(5),   x3    = rnorm(5) ) preds <- sae_predict(fit, newdata = new_areas, allow_new_levels = TRUE) preds $verdict [1] "All chains converged: Rhat < 1.01, ESS > 400, no divergent transitions."    group estimate  lower  upper 1   101   0.524  0.461  0.589 2   102   0.461  0.395  0.531 3   103   0.587  0.523  0.648 4   104   0.448  0.378  0.519 5   105   0.495  0.432  0.560

title: Binomial Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: Use hbm_binlogitnorm() raw success counts known trial counts. Use hbm_betalogitnorm() instead direct proportion estimates design effect. Add spatial structure via spatial_var, spatial_model, M – see spatial vignette. Missing data auto-handled multiple imputation (mice).

title: Binomial Logit-Normal SAE Model
text: Rao, J. N. K., & Molina, . (2015). Small Area Estimation, 2nd ed. Wiley. Chapter 4 covers area-level binary SAE. Buerkner, P.-C. (2017). brms: R Package Bayesian Multilevel Models Using Stan. Journal Statistical Software, 80(1), 1-28.

what: The Bayesian principle: response variables are never imputed
title: Handling Missing Data in hbsaems
text: core design rule hbsaems multiple imputation ever imputes auxiliary (predictor) variables, never response. limitation – statistically correct Bayesian treatment: Bayesian hierarchical model response YY, posterior parameters θ\theta marginalises missing outcomes YmisY_{\text{mis}}: p(θ∣Yobs,X)=∫p(θ∣Yobs,Ymis,X)p(Ymis∣Yobs,X,θ)dYmis. p(\theta \mid Y_{\text{obs}}, X) = \int p(\theta \mid Y_{\text{obs}},   Y_{\text{mis}}, X) \, p(Y_{\text{mis}} \mid Y_{\text{obs}}, X, \theta)   \, \mathrm{d}Y_{\text{mis}}. Plugging mice-imputed value YY replace integral single point substitute, deflate posterior uncertainty, bias estimates imputation model misspecified. correct way “fill ” missing YY values joint Bayesian imputation via brms::mi(), samples YmisY_{\text{mis}} alongside θ\theta. handle_missing = "model" strategy described . three strategies thus different scopes: handle_missing = NULL (default), package auto-selects strategy based family’s supports_mi flag.

title: Handling Missing Data in hbsaems
text: Three patterns common: Missing response, complete covariates – areas sampled , administrative auxiliaries . Complete response, missing covariates – survey reached every area, census variable missing . Missing – mix . package’s reaction pattern depends handle_missing strategy effect. next three sections walk strategy patterns handles.

title: Handling Missing Data in hbsaems
text: handle_missing = "deleted": Rows response YY missing dropped model fitting. auxiliary missing, function refuses informative error – listwise deletion XX discard information principled strategies can recover. pass data missing XX "deleted", call rejected: dropped rows (legitimate Y-case) retained fit_deleted$data sae_predict() can still produce predictions areas using auxiliary values.
code: library(hbsaems) data("data_fhnorm")  # Pattern 1: missing Y only, X complete data_miss_y      <- data_fhnorm data_miss_y$y[c(3, 14, 27)] <- NA  fit_deleted <- hbm(   formula        = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   re             = ~ (1 | regency),   data           = data_miss_y,   handle_missing = "deleted",   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 1 ) handle_missing = 'deleted': 3 row(s) with missing response variable removed from model fitting. data_miss_x <- data_fhnorm data_miss_x$x1[6:8] <- NA  hbm(brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),     data           = data_miss_x,     re             = ~ (1 | regency),     handle_missing = "deleted") Error: Option `handle_missing = 'deleted'` requires all auxiliary (predictor) variables to be complete. Missing values were detected in: x1. Consider `handle_missing = 'multiple'` to impute missing predictors.

title: Handling Missing Data in hbsaems
text: handle_missing = "multiple", mice imputes missing auxiliary variables mm times, fits brms model imputed dataset, pools posterior draws via Rubin’s rules (via brms::brm_multiple). response never imputed mice. package handles pattern:

what: Pattern 2a: Missing X only – normal mice workflow
title: Handling Missing Data in hbsaems
text: pooled posterior returned usual hbmfit interface. Diagnostics per-imputation; global Rhat / ESS view informative pooled chains – see ?brm_multiple details.
code: data_miss_x         <- data_fhnorm data_miss_x$x1[6:8] <- NA  fit_mi <- hbm(   formula        = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   re             = ~ (1 | regency),   data           = data_miss_x,   handle_missing = "multiple",   m              = 5,                       # 5 imputations   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 1 ) Missing predictor variable(s): x1. Applying multiple imputation (mice) with m = 5 imputations.  Compiling the C++ model Fitting imputed model 1 Start sampling ... Fitting imputed model 5 Start sampling ...  (Posterior draws pooled via Rubin's rules.)

what: Pattern 2b: Missing X and Y (or only Y) – response not imputed
title: Handling Missing Data in hbsaems
text: YY also missing values, package issues warning explaining mice impute YY rows missing YY excluded fitting retained returned object:
code: data_miss_both <- data_fhnorm data_miss_both$y[2:3]   <- NA           # missing Y data_miss_both$x1[6:8]  <- NA           # missing X  fit_mi_both <- hbm(   formula        = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   re             = ~ (1 | regency),   data           = data_miss_both,   handle_missing = "multiple" ) Warning: Missing values detected in response variable(s): y. Multiple imputation via `mice` applies ONLY to auxiliary predictor variables (X). Response variable(s) are NEVER imputed inside a Bayesian model -- missing Y rows will be excluded from model fitting but are retained in the returned object for prediction via hbsae(). If you wish to model missingness in Y jointly with the parameters, consider `handle_missing = 'model'` (continuous outcomes only).  Missing predictor variable(s): x1. Applying multiple imputation (mice) with m = 5 imputations.

title: Handling Missing Data in hbsaems
text: handle_missing = "multiple" given Y missing (XX complete), nothing mice impute. package auto-converts appropriate strategy: Continuous family (e.g. gaussian, lognormal): auto-converts handle_missing = "model" adds | mi() formula LHS missing YY values jointly sampled parameters (correct Bayesian approach). Discrete family (e.g. binomial, poisson): mi() supported – falls back handle_missing = "deleted", dropping rows missing YY.
code: data_miss_y_only <- data_fhnorm data_miss_y_only$y[2:3] <- NA           # only Y missing  # Gaussian family -> auto-converts to "model" with | mi() on LHS fit_auto1 <- hbm(   formula        = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   re             = ~ (1 | regency),   data           = data_miss_y_only,   handle_missing = "multiple" ) handle_missing = 'multiple' was specified but only the response variable (Y) is missing and X is complete. mice imputes predictor variables only and cannot impute Y. Automatically converting to handle_missing = 'model': '| mi()' will be added to the formula LHS so that brms jointly estimates missing Y with the model parameters (the correct Bayesian approach).

title: Handling Missing Data in hbsaems
text: Pass options mice_args:
code: fit_mi2 <- hbm(   formula        = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   re             = ~ (1 | regency),   data           = data_miss_x,   handle_missing = "multiple",   m              = 10,   mice_args      = list(method = "pmm", maxit = 20),   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 1 )

title: Handling Missing Data in hbsaems
text: handle_missing = "model", missing values response auxiliary variables sampled jointly model parameters via brms::mi(). user must specify imputation sub-models explicitly formula: y | mi() ~ ... tells brms treat missing YY values parameters sampled. mi(x1) RHS tells brms use imputed x1x_1 (also sampled) predictor. second bf(x1 | mi() ~ x2 + x3) imputation sub-model x1x_1. result multivariate brms model missing values sampled alongside regression coefficients; posterior uncertainty imputations automatically propagated. Restriction: handle_missing = "model" works continuous distributions (supports_mi flag family registry). discrete families (binomial, Poisson, …), brms::mi() supported – use "multiple" missing XX accept listwise deletion missing YY.
code: data_miss_both <- data_fhnorm data_miss_both$y[2:3]   <- NA data_miss_both$x1[6:8]  <- NA  fit_model <- hbm(   formula        = brms::bf(y  | mi() ~ mi(x1) + x2 + x3) +                    brms::bf(x1 | mi() ~ x2 + x3),   re             = ~ (1 | regency),   data           = data_miss_both,   handle_missing = "model",   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 1 ) Compiling Stan program... Start sampling...

title: Handling Missing Data in hbsaems
text: omit handle_missing, package looks whether missingness exists selects based family’s supports_mi flag: actual decision rule used code : auto-selection intentionally simple branch “Y-” vs “X-” patterns: subtleties handled inside chosen strategy (see Pattern 2c case "multiple" detects Y-missingness auto-converts "model" continuous families "deleted" discrete families). can always override passing explicit handle_missing = "...".
code: # Build a data frame with some missing values to demonstrate data("data_lnln") data_with_some_na <- data_lnln data_with_some_na$x1[c(3, 14, 27)] <- NA    # missing covariate  fit_auto <- hbm_lnln(   response  = "y_obs",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var  = "district",   data      = data_with_some_na,   # handle_missing not given   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 1 ) Missing values detected. Auto-selecting handle_missing = 'multiple' (family 'lognormal', supports_mi = FALSE). Any NA detected in response or auxiliary?   └── family.supports_mi == TRUE?   ->  "model"   (joint mi(); user must   │                                                supply mi() formula   │                                                terms when missing X)   └── otherwise                      ->  "multiple" (mice on X only;                                                      missing Y handled                                                      per Strategy 2 logic)

what: Comparing strategies (when fitting all three is feasible)
title: Handling Missing Data in hbsaems
text: Note fit_deleted fit_mi refer fits pattern (missing Y , missing X respectively), comparison comparable likelihood scales.
code: loo_compare <- loo::loo_compare(   loo::loo(fit_deleted$brmsfit),   loo::loo(fit_mi$brmsfit),   loo::loo(fit_model$brmsfit) ) loo_compare elpd_diff se_diff fit_model         0.0       0.0 fit_mi           -2.1       2.4 fit_deleted     -10.7       3.8

title: Handling Missing Data in hbsaems
text: Small fraction missing (< 5%) likely MCAR, missing Y – "deleted" fine. Continuous family – prefer "model" (joint Bayesian imputation, principled). Discrete family – use "multiple" (mice X; rows missing Y dropped fitting retained result prediction). Missing response , administrative auxiliaries areas – prefer "deleted" plus sae_predict() (transparent imputing response). Reproducibility – set seed wrapper call (using mice) within mice_args make imputations reproducible.

title: Handling Missing Data in hbsaems
text: Rubin, D. B. (1987). Multiple Imputation Nonresponse Surveys. Wiley. van Buuren, S. (2018). Flexible Imputation Missing Data. CRC Press. Bürkner, P.-C. (2017). brms: R Package Bayesian Multilevel Models Using Stan. Journal Statistical Software, 80(1), 1-28. Section mi(). Gelman, ., & Hill, J. (2006). Data Analysis Using Regression Multilevel/Hierarchical Models. Cambridge University Press, Chapter 25.

what: When to use the lognormal-lognormal model
title: Lognormal-Lognormal SAE Model
text: great many positive, right-skewed survey responses well approximated lognormal distribution: household expenditure, agricultural yield, firm revenue, length hospital stay. Modelling log response normal hierarchy workhorse choice SAE (Slud & Maiti 2006; & Chapman 2006). hbm_lnln() fits yi∣θi,σ∼Lognormal(θi,σ2),θi=xi⊤𝛃+ui,ui∼𝒩(0,σu2), \begin{aligned} y_i \mid \theta_i, \sigma    &\sim \mathrm{Lognormal}(\theta_i, \sigma^2), \\ \theta_i    &= x_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta} + u_i, \quad      u_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_u^2), \end{aligned} .e. log-mean parameter θi\theta_i linear auxiliaries plus IID area random effect, within-area residual variance σ2\sigma^2 either sampled (default) pinned known sampling variance survey design.

title: Lognormal-Lognormal SAE Model
text: y_obs direct survey estimate (positive); psi_i known per-area sampling variance log scale, .e. variance log⁡(ŷ)\log(\hat y_i). survey software reports Var(ŷ)\mathrm{Var}(\hat y_i) original scale, convert delta-method approximation ψi≈Var(ŷ)/ŷi2 \psi_i \approx \mathrm{Var}(\hat y_i) \, / \, \hat y_i^2  passing column.

title: Lognormal-Lognormal SAE Model
text: sigma appears “Distributional Parameters” block – sampled.
code: library(hbsaems) fit <- hbm_lnln(   response  = "y_obs",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var   = "district",   data      = data_lnln,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4,   seed = 1 ) summary(fit) ===== Hierarchical Bayesian Model Summary =====   Observations : 100  Family       : lognormal (link: identity )  Formula      : y_obs ~ x1 + x2 + x3 + (1 | district)  ----- Parameter Estimates -----  Family: lognormal   Links: mu = identity; sigma = log Formula: y_obs ~ x1 + x2 + x3 + (1 | district)    Data: data_lnln (Number of observations: 100)   Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 4000; warmup = 2000; thin = 1;          total post-warmup draws = 8000  Multilevel Hyperparameters: ~group (Number of levels: 100)               Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS sd(Intercept)     0.21      0.04     0.13     0.29 1.00     2418     3214  Regression Coefficients:           Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS Intercept     1.51      0.06     1.39     1.63 1.00     4912     5827 x1            0.32      0.04     0.24     0.40 1.00     6204     6519 x2           -0.18      0.04    -0.26    -0.10 1.00     5891     5934 x3            0.11      0.03     0.05     0.17 1.00     6308     6147  Further Distributional Parameters:       Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS sigma     0.27      0.03     0.21     0.34 1.00     5417     6028  Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS).

what: Mode 2: Fay-Herriot variant with known sampling variance
title: Lognormal-Lognormal SAE Model
text: know per-area sampling variance ψi\psi_i survey design (classic Fay-Herriot situation log scale), pass sampling_variance: Implementation note (v1.0.0). hood, hbm_lnln() stores ψi\sqrt{\psi_i} hidden offset column attaches sigma ~ 0 + offset(.hbsaems_sigma_fixed) brms formula. brms’s default link_sigma = "log" otherwise apply exp⁡(⋅)\exp(\cdot) offset value (producing σi=exp⁡(ψi)\sigma_i = \exp(\sqrt{\psi_i}) instead ψi\sqrt{\psi_i}), hbsaems automatically overrides link "identity" whenever distributional parameter pinned via sampling_variance fixed_params. v1.0.0 fix; earlier development versions silently miscalibrated model. Notice : sigma longer listed output – pinned ψi\sqrt{\psi_i} per area. sd(Intercept) (area-level variance σu\sigma_u) shrinks σ\sigma longer competing residual variability. lognormal version Fay-Herriot model.
code: fit_fh <- hbm_lnln(   response     = "y_obs",   auxiliary    = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var   = "district",   sampling_variance = "psi_i",     # <- pins sigma_i = sqrt(psi_i)   data         = data_lnln,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4,   seed = 1 ) summary(fit_fh) ===== Hierarchical Bayesian Model Summary =====   Observations : 100  Family       : lognormal (link: identity )  Formula      : y_obs ~ x1 + x2 + x3 + (1 | district)                 sigma ~ 0 + offset(.hbsaems_sigma_fixed)  Multilevel Hyperparameters: ~group (Number of levels: 100)               Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS sd(Intercept)     0.18      0.03     0.12     0.25 1.00     2843     3415  Regression Coefficients:           Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS Intercept     1.51      0.05     1.41     1.61 1.00     5012     5783 x1            0.32      0.04     0.24     0.40 1.00     6204     6519 x2           -0.18      0.04    -0.26    -0.10 1.00     5891     5934 x3            0.11      0.03     0.05     0.17 1.00     6308     6147

title: Lognormal-Lognormal SAE Model
text: want σ\sigma sampled (Mode 1) tighter prior brms’s default: default priors classes (Intercept, b, sd(Intercept)) remain effect.
code: fit_custom <- hbm_lnln(   response  = "y_obs",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var   = "district",   data      = data_lnln,   prior     = brms::set_prior("normal(0.3, 0.05)", class = "sigma"),   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4,   seed = 1 )

title: Lognormal-Lognormal SAE Model
text: Equivalent Mode 2 via generic interface: formula ~ sqrt(psi_i) evaluated data, producing length-nrow(data) vector pinned values.

title: Lognormal-Lognormal SAE Model
text: Suppose x1 non-linear effect. Add thin-plate spline: formula becomes y_obs ~ s(x1) + x2 + x3 + (1 | district). See vignette("advanced-features") full coverage nonlinear terms.
code: fit_spline <- hbm_lnln(   response          = "y_obs",   auxiliary         = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var          = "district",   sampling_variance = "psi_i",   data              = data_lnln,   nonlinear         = "x1",   nonlinear_type    = "spline",   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 1 )

title: Lognormal-Lognormal SAE Model
text: lognormal family models log⁡y\log y; predictions returned original (back-transformed) scale sae_predict(): Posterior means back-transformed correctly (taking Jensen inequality account, since E[exp⁡(θ+u)]≠exp⁡(E[θ+u])E[\exp(\theta + u)] \neq \exp(E[\theta + u])).
code: new_areas <- data.frame(   group = 101:105,   x1    = rnorm(5),   x2    = rnorm(5),   x3    = rnorm(5) ) preds <- sae_predict(fit_fh, newdata = new_areas, allow_new_levels = TRUE) preds group estimate  lower upper 1   101    5.23   3.92  6.91 2   102    4.18   3.10  5.62 3   103    6.04   4.51  8.04 4   104    3.71   2.71  4.99 5   105    4.95   3.71  6.59

title: Lognormal-Lognormal SAE Model
text: hbm_lnln() covers positive right-skewed survey responses lognormal likelihood. Pin σ\sigma via sampling_variance = "psi_i" get Fay-Herriot variant – usually preferred survey design info reliable. Use prior argument tune σ\sigma class. Nonlinear smooths via nonlinear nonlinear_type.

text: Slud, E. V., & Maiti, T. (2006). Mean-squared error estimation transformed Fay-Herriot models. JRSSB 68(2), 239-257. , Y., & Chapman, B. (2006). Small area estimation using area level models estimated sampling variances. Survey Methodology 32(1), 97-103. Rao, J. N. K., & Molina, . (2015). Small Area Estimation, 2nd ed. Wiley.

what: Why a dedicated package for Small Area Estimation?
title: Modelling Workflow Overview with hbsaems
text: Small Area Estimation (SAE) addresses common survey problem: official surveys designed produce reliable estimates large geographic demographic levels (national, provincial), planners often need estimates much finer levels (district, kecamatan, ethnic subgroup, short time period) direct survey estimates noisy. Hierarchical Bayesian SAE family models works borrowing strength across areas hierarchical model: yi=xi⊤𝛃+ui+ei,ui∼𝒩(0,σu2),ei∼𝒩(0,ψi) y_i = x_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta} + u_i + e_i, \qquad u_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_u^2), \qquad e_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \psi_i) ψi\psi_i known sampling variance survey design (Fay-Herriot signature) uiu_i random area effect pulls noisy direct estimates toward regression-based prediction. hbsaems thin, opinionated layer brms gives SAE practitioners: small set convenience wrappers (hbm_lnln, hbm_betalogitnorm, hbm_binlogitnorm) arguments named SAE concepts (auxiliary, sampling_variance, n + deff); generic factory hbm_flex() handles registered likelihood; universal entry point hbm() fully custom models including custom brms response distributions; helpers convergence diagnostics, model comparison, --sample prediction unsampled areas. vignette high-level tour. family-specific vignettes (hbsaems-lnln-model, hbsaems-betalogitnorm-model, hbsaems-binlogitnorm-model, hbsaems-spatial, hbsaems-handle-missing) drill model class.

what: The three layers of the function family
title: Modelling Workflow Overview with hbsaems
text: users start Layer 1: pick wrapper matches response distribution, plug data, get fit. need arises wrapper doesn’t cover (exotic family, custom prior nuisance parameter), step Layer 2 Layer 3.
code: LAYER 3:  hbm()                       Universal: any brms family, full customisation                                        ▲                                        |                       LAYER 2:  hbm_flex()                       Family registry, auxiliary, fixed_params                                        ▲                                        |         LAYER 1:  Wrappers (hbm_lnln, hbm_betalogitnorm, hbm_binlogitnorm)         SAE-friendly: response, auxiliary, area_var, n/deff, sampling_variance

what: Quick example: a Fay-Herriot lognormal model
title: Modelling Workflow Overview with hbsaems
text: use bundled data_lnln simulated dataset. mimics typical survey output: direct estimator y_obs, known sampling variance psi_i, area-level auxiliary variables x1, x2, x3, area identifier group. simplest call (everything defaults; sigma random): Fay-Herriot variant sigma pinned known sampling variance psi_i: Either fit returns hbmfit object downstream interface:
code: data("data_lnln") str(data_lnln[, c("district", "y_obs", "psi_i", "x1", "x2", "x3")]) #> 'data.frame':    100 obs. of  6 variables: #>  $ district: chr  "district_001" "district_002" "district_003" "district_004" ... #>  $ y_obs   : num  4.5 2.98 2.5 1.97 2.04 ... #>  $ psi_i   : num  0.1222 0.0725 0.046 0.1003 0.0943 ... #>  $ x1      : num  -0.5605 -0.2302 1.5587 0.0705 0.1293 ... #>  $ x2      : num  0.2896 1.2569 0.7533 0.6525 0.0484 ... #>  $ x3      : num  1.199 0.312 -1.265 -0.457 -1.414 ... 'data.frame':   100 obs. of  6 variables:  $ district: chr  "district_001" "district_002" "district_003" "district_004" ...  $ y_obs: num  4.21 3.18 6.55 5.04 7.12 ...  $ psi_i: num  0.058 0.071 0.044 0.089 0.062 ...  $ x1   : num  1.34 -0.42 0.51 -1.07 0.89 ...  $ x2   : num  -0.79 1.21 -0.40 0.55 -0.30 ...  $ x3   : num  0.07 0.94 -0.92 1.07 0.71 ... library(hbsaems) fit <- hbm_lnln(   response  = "y_obs",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var   = "district",   data      = data_lnln,   chains    = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4,   seed      = 1 ) fit_fh <- hbm_lnln(   response     = "y_obs",   auxiliary    = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var   = "district",   sampling_variance = "psi_i",     # <- pins sigma_i = sqrt(psi_i)   data         = data_lnln,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 1 ) summary(fit_fh) ===== Hierarchical Bayesian Model Summary =====   Observations : 100  Family       : lognormal (link: identity )  Formula      : y_obs ~ x1 + x2 + x3 + (1 | district)  ----- Parameter Estimates -----  Family: lognormal   Links: mu = identity; sigma = log Formula: y_obs ~ x1 + x2 + x3 + (1 | district)    Data: data_lnln (Number of observations: 100)   Draws: 4 chains, each with iter = 4000; warmup = 2000; thin = 1;          total post-warmup draws = 8000  Multilevel Hyperparameters: ~group (Number of levels: 100)               Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS sd(Intercept)     0.18      0.04     0.10     0.27 1.00     2143     2841  Regression Coefficients:           Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS Intercept     1.51      0.05     1.41     1.61 1.00     5012     5783 x1            0.32      0.04     0.24     0.40 1.00     6204     6519 x2           -0.18      0.04    -0.26    -0.10 1.00     5891     5934 x3            0.11      0.03     0.05     0.17 1.00     6308     6147  Draws were sampled using sampling(NUTS).  For each parameter, Bulk_ESS and Tail_ESS are effective sample size measures, and Rhat is the potential scale reduction factor on split chains (at convergence, Rhat = 1).

what: When to step up to hbm_flex() or hbm()
title: Modelling Workflow Overview with hbsaems
text: Use hbm_flex() : need likelihood wrappers don’t expose (e.g. lognormal logit-link mu, custom registered family); want pin nuisance parameter generic fixed_params interface; need fine control priors via stanvars. Use hbm() : arbitrarily complex brms::bf() formula; need multivariate response mi() imputation; working custom brms family object directly; need control every brms argument. three layers share consistent interface (auxiliary, group, spatial_var, M, fixed_params, prior, stanvars), moving hierarchy mostly additive – keep arguments already gain extras.

Programmers prototyping model: quick way explore effect priors, link functions, spatial structure small dataset committing scripted workflow. app meant replace scripted modelling production work – audit trail .R script essential .

what: Why spatial structure matters in SAE
title: Spatial SAE Models: CAR, SAR, BYM2
text: Many SAE responses exhibit spatial autocorrelation: neighbouring districts Indonesia (kecamatan within kabupaten) tend similar literacy rates, poverty rates, vaccination coverage share population characteristics, infrastructure, administrative culture. non-spatial random-intercept model ui∼𝒩(0,σu2)u_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_u^2) treats area random effects exchangeable – area 7 area 8 similar area 7 area 247. areas geographic adjacency, independence assumption discards real information. Spatial random-effects models replace IID u multivariate distribution encodes neighbourhood structure. hbsaems exposes three standard choices one consistent interface:

title: Spatial SAE Models: CAR, SAR, BYM2
text: spatial models hbsaems consume square binary row-normalised weight matrix MM Mij=1M_{ij} = 1 (positive weight) iff areas ii jj neighbours. package ships two example matrices: build shapefile, use package’s build_spatial_weight() helper: matrix checked symmetry, zero rows, basic validity check_spatial_weight(). See ?build_spatial_weight KNN, distance-band, contiguity methods.
code: data("adjacency_matrix_car") dim(adjacency_matrix_car) #> [1] 5 5 data("spatial_weight_sar") dim(spatial_weight_sar) #> [1] 100 100 [1] 100 100 [1] 100 100 # Example workflow (sf must be installed) library(sf) shp <- st_read("kecamatan.shp", quiet = TRUE) W   <- build_spatial_weight(shp,                             neighbour_method = "queen",                             standardise      = "row")

what: Choosing between re, spatial_var, or both
title: Spatial SAE Models: CAR, SAR, BYM2
text: hbsaems exposes two distinct argument families area-level random effects: re = ~ (1 | area) – standard IID area random effect, .e. classical Fay-Herriot ui∼𝒩(0,σu2)u_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma_u^2). spatial_var = "area" spatial_model = "car" "sar" – spatially structured random effect uses adjacency / weight matrix MM. Either one alone sufficient valid SAE model. Use whichever matches scientific assumption neighbouring areas relate: Key rule thumb: BYM2 already includes IID component internally, set car_type = "bym2" need add re separately. Providing fits older parallel BYM specification instead; hbsaems prints advisory message recommending BYM2. plain CAR / SAR (without BYM2), spatial effect typically carries entire area-level variance. Adding IID re grouping unusual.

title: Spatial SAE Models: CAR, SAR, BYM2
text: simplest specification: pass spatial_var, spatial_model = "car", M. intrinsic CAR (ICAR) used default one fewer parameter computationally cheaper: new diagnostic interest sdcar – standard deviation spatial random effect. sdcar much smaller IID random-intercept SD non-spatial fit, spatial signal weak.
code: library(hbsaems) data("data_fhnorm") data("adjacency_matrix_car")  fit_icar <- hbm(   formula           = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   hb_sampling       = "gaussian",   spatial_var       = "province",   spatial_model     = "car",   M                 = adjacency_matrix_car,   data              = data_fhnorm,   sampling_variance = "D",                       # known sampling variance   control           = list(adapt_delta = 0.99),   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4,   seed = 1 ) summary(fit_icar) ===== Hierarchical Bayesian Model Summary =====   Observations : 100  Family       : gaussian (link: identity )  Formula      : y ~ x1 + x2 + x3 + car(M, gr = province, type = "icar")  ----- Parameter Estimates ----- Correlation Structures:       Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS sdcar     0.31      0.07     0.18     0.46 1.00     1742     2419  Regression Coefficients:           Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS Intercept     1.72      0.12     1.49     1.95 1.00     5208     5917 x1            0.45      0.04     0.37     0.53 1.00     6418     6628 x2           -0.33      0.03    -0.39    -0.27 1.00     6021     6105 x3            0.19      0.02     0.15     0.23 1.00     6304     6207

title: Spatial SAE Models: CAR, SAR, BYM2
text: want proper (intrinsic) CAR – e.g. compute Bayes factor non-spatial alternative – use car_type = "escar": adds extra car mixing parameter (0 1) sampled alongside sdcar.
code: fit_escar <- hbm(   formula           = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   spatial_var       = "province",   spatial_model     = "car",   car_type          = "escar",   M                 = adjacency_matrix_car,   data              = data_fhnorm,   sampling_variance = "D",   control           = list(adapt_delta = 0.99),   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 1 )

title: Spatial SAE Models: CAR, SAR, BYM2
text: Riebler et al. (2016) recommend BYM2 modern default: parameterises convolution spatial CAR IID random effect mixing weight ρ∈[0,1]\rho \[0, 1]: ui=σu(ρ⋅vi+1−ρ⋅ϵi),u_i = \sigma_u \left( \sqrt{\rho} \cdot v_i + \sqrt{1 - \rho} \cdot \epsilon_i \right), viv_i ICAR-distributed ϵi\epsilon_i IID standard normal. decorrelates two variance components gives principled prior ρ\rho. BYM2 reparameterisation internally combines IID effect ICAR effect via mixing weight ρ\rho. therefore need add re = ~ (1 | province) separately – fits different (less identifiable) parallel BYM specification. supply , hbsaems prints advisory message pointing BYM2 parameterisation preferred alternative.
code: fit_bym2 <- hbm(   formula           = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   spatial_var       = "province",   spatial_model     = "car",   car_type          = "bym2",                          # BYM2 already includes IID + CAR   M                 = adjacency_matrix_car,   data              = data_fhnorm,   sampling_variance = "D",   control           = list(adapt_delta = 0.99),   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 1 )

title: Spatial SAE Models: CAR, SAR, BYM2
text: Simultaneous autoregressive models specify u=ρsWu+ε,u = \rho_s W u + \varepsilon, .e. uiu_i linear combination neighbours’ effects plus iid shock. Use spatial_model = "sar": lagsar parameter spatial autocorrelation coefficient ρs\rho_s. Use sar_type = "error" (rather default "lag") SAR error model.
code: data("spatial_weight_sar") # `spatial_weight_sar` is a 100x100 row-standardised matrix with # rownames regency_001..regency_100; it pairs with the *fine* # "regency" column (100 levels) of data_fhnorm.  Using # spatial_var = "province" here would mismatch the matrix # dimension (5 provinces vs 100 matrix rows). fit_sar <- hbm(   formula           = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   spatial_var       = "regency",   spatial_model     = "sar",   M                 = spatial_weight_sar,   data              = data_fhnorm,   sampling_variance = "D",   control           = list(adapt_delta = 0.99),   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 1 ) summary(fit_sar) ===== Hierarchical Bayesian Model Summary =====   Family       : gaussian (link: identity )  Formula      : y ~ x1 + x2 + x3 + sar(M, gr = province, type = "lag")  Correlation Structures:        Estimate Est.Error l-95% CI u-95% CI Rhat Bulk_ESS Tail_ESS lagsar     0.18      0.11    -0.04     0.40 1.00     2418     3127 sdsar      0.41      0.09     0.25     0.61 1.00     2517     3104

title: Spatial SAE Models: CAR, SAR, BYM2
text: spatial_var, spatial_model, M arguments work wrappers:
code: # Beta + CAR fit_beta_car <- hbm_betalogitnorm(   response  = "y", auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   n         = "n", deff = "deff",   spatial_var = "province", spatial_model = "car", M = adjacency_matrix_car,   data      = data_betalogitnorm,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 1 )  # Binomial + BYM2 # data_binlogitnorm uses "regency" (5 levels) as its coarse spatial # cluster, paired with adjacency_matrix_car_regency.  (See R/data.R # top comment for the naming convention.) fit_bin_bym2 <- hbm_binlogitnorm(   response  = "y", trials = "n",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   spatial_var = "regency", spatial_model = "car", car_type = "bym2",   M         = adjacency_matrix_car_regency,   data      = data_binlogitnorm,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 1 )

title: Spatial SAE Models: CAR, SAR, BYM2
text: Bad weight matrices #1 cause slow / divergent spatial fits. Always inspect: non-zero n_isolates (rows neighbours) break CAR; add tiny diagonal merge isolates nearby area.
code: check_spatial_weight(adjacency_matrix_car) $is_square [1] TRUE  $is_symmetric [1] TRUE  $n_isolates [1] 0  $mean_neighbours [1] 5.2  $verdict [1] "Weight matrix passes basic checks."

what: Comparing spatial vs non-spatial fits with LOO
title: Spatial SAE Models: CAR, SAR, BYM2
text: BYM2 preferred ; ICAR essentially indistinguishable BYM2 (elpd_diff well within 1 SE), decisively beat IID baseline.
code: fit_iid <- hbm(formula           = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),                re                = ~ (1 | province),                data              = data_fhnorm,                sampling_variance = "D",                control           = list(adapt_delta = 0.99),                chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 4, seed = 1)  loo_compare <- loo::loo_compare(   loo::loo(fit_iid$brmsfit),   loo::loo(fit_icar$brmsfit),   loo::loo(fit_bym2$brmsfit) ) loo_compare elpd_diff se_diff fit_bym2          0.0       0.0 fit_icar         -1.2       1.8 fit_iid          -8.4       3.1

title: Spatial SAE Models: CAR, SAR, BYM2
text: Use spatial_model = "car" (default ICAR) SAE workhorse spatial prior. Use car_type = "bym2" (alone, without separate re term) modern Riebler et al. parameterisation, internally combines IID CAR via single mixing weight. SAR (spatial_model = "sar") appropriate spatial mechanism regression-like (current area depends neighbours’ levels). Always validate weight matrix fitting.

title: Spatial SAE Models: CAR, SAR, BYM2
text: Besag, J., York, J., & Mollie, . (1991). Bayesian image restoration, two applications spatial statistics. Annals Institute Statistical Mathematics 43, 1-20. Riebler, ., Sorbye, S. H., Simpson, D., & Rue, H. (2016). intuitive Bayesian spatial model disease mapping accounts scaling. Statistical Methods Medical Research 25(4), 1145-1165. Anselin, L. (1988). Spatial Econometrics: Methods Models. Kluwer Academic Publishers.

title: Migration Guide: legacy v0.1.x names to v1.0.0
text: Behaviour change must aware upgrading v1.0.x. sae_predict() now defaults predict_type = "epred". summarises posterior area mean rather posterior predictive new observation . correct small-area-estimation target, reported SD / RSE_percent now smaller v1.0.x (longer include observation-level likelihood variance). Every RSE number existing scripts produce change. reproduce old values exactly: hbm() default adapt_delta now 0.95 (brms’s 0.8). Hierarchical SAE funnels need higher value avoid spurious divergences. control value set still respected. model_compare() / model_compare_all() now check Pareto-k PSIS-LOO reliability previously inert reloo_args argument functional – supplying triggers brms::reloo() high-k folds. convergence_check() now reports divergent transitions, E-BFMI, max-treedepth hit rate numbers summary().
code: # v1.0.x behaviour (posterior predictive of a new observation) est <- sae_predict(model, predict_type = "response")

title: Migration Guide: legacy v0.1.x names to v1.0.0
text: Function names modernised follow brms snake_case convention. original short-form names (hbcc, hbmc, hbpc, hbsae) now deprecated – still work produce warning per session. deprecated names removed v2.0.0.

title: Migration Guide: legacy v0.1.x names to v1.0.0
code: # Before (legacy v0.1.x names) diag  <- hbcc(model) comp  <- hbmc(model, model2) pc    <- hbpc(model_prior, data, "y") est   <- hbsae(model)  # After (v1.0.0 names) diag  <- convergence_check(model) comp  <- model_compare(model, model2) pc    <- prior_check(model_prior, data = data, response_var = "y") est   <- sae_predict(model)

title: Migration Guide: legacy v0.1.x names to v1.0.0
text: project root:
code: sed -i "s/hbcc(/convergence_check(/g"  *.R sed -i "s/hbmc(/model_compare(/g"      *.R sed -i "s/hbpc(/prior_check(/g"        *.R sed -i "s/hbsae(/sae_predict(/g"       *.R

title: Migration Guide: legacy v0.1.x names to v1.0.0
text: addition function renames, several argument names modernised hbm() wrapper functions (hbm_lnln(), hbm_betalogitnorm(), hbm_binlogitnorm()). classical SAE literature describes spatial random effect “area” variable, generic “group”, new names spell : old names continue work one release cycle emit soft deprecation warning. scheduled removal v2.0.0. wrapper functions:
code: # Old (still works in v1.0.0, but emits a warning) fit <- hbm(formula = bf(y ~ x1 + x2),            sre     = "province",            sre_type = "car",            car_type = "bym2",            M       = adjacency_matrix_car,            data    = data_fhnorm)  # New (v1.0.0 canonical) fit <- hbm(formula       = bf(y ~ x1 + x2),            spatial_var   = "province",            spatial_model = "car",            car_type      = "bym2",            M             = adjacency_matrix_car,            data          = data_fhnorm) # Old (still works, emits warning) fit <- hbm_lnln(response  = "y_obs",                 auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),                 group     = "district",     # deprecated                 data      = data_lnln)  # New fit <- hbm_lnln(response  = "y_obs",                 auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),                 area_var  = "district",     # NEW                 data      = data_lnln)

title: Migration Guide: legacy v0.1.x names to v1.0.0
text: bundled toy datasets renamed reflect concrete Indonesian administrative levels, making easier recognise SAE roles column: matrix adjacency_matrix_car relabelled row-/column-names province_01 .. province_05. new companion matrix adjacency_matrix_car_regency (rownames regency_01 .. regency_05) ships alongside district-level datasets.

title: Migration Guide: legacy v0.1.x names to v1.0.0
code: library(hbsaems) data("data_fhnorm")  model  <- hbm(brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3), data = data_fhnorm,               re                = ~ (1 | regency),               sampling_variance = "D",                 # Fay-Herriot identifiability               control           = list(adapt_delta = 0.99),               chains = 4, iter = 4000) model2 <- hbm(brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2),      data = data_fhnorm,               re                = ~ (1 | regency),               sampling_variance = "D",               control           = list(adapt_delta = 0.99),               chains = 4, iter = 4000)  # Quick convergence check is_converged(model) is_converged(model, threshold = 1.05)  # Full diagnostics diag <- convergence_check(model) summary(diag) plot(diag, type = "trace")  # Multi-model ranking (analogous to loo_compare in brms) tbl <- model_compare_all(full   = model,                          medium = model2,                          criterion = "both") print(tbl)  # Bayesian model averaging avg_est <- model_average(model, model2, weights = c(0.6, 0.4))  # Standard S3 methods now work on hbmfit coef(model);  fixef(model);  nobs(model) predict(model);  fitted(model) update(model, iter = 8000)  # Posterior extraction draws <- posterior_draws(model) ci95  <- posterior_interval(model, prob = 0.95)  # Inspection helpers hbm_info(model) hbm_warnings(model)

title: Migration Guide: legacy v0.1.x names to v1.0.0
text: following items round v1.0.0 release. listed separately introduced initial section stabilised.

title: Migration Guide: legacy v0.1.x names to v1.0.0
text: Earlier development versions: weights = NULL default rep(1/n, n), silently assumed target population mean. users passed population total, resulting benchmarked estimates scale-corrupt factor nn. Current v1.0.0 behaviour: new argument target_type = c("total", "mean") chooses safe default. default "total" (BPS convention), weights = NULL now means rep(1, n). v1.0.0 code passed population mean without explicit weights, add target_type = "mean" retain old default.
code: # OLD code (silently used 1/n; corrupt if target is a total) sae_benchmark(predictions, target = 1e6)  # NEW (explicit, correct) sae_benchmark(predictions, target = 1e6, target_type = "total") # or — recommended for production — sae_benchmark(predictions, target = 1e6, weights = popsize_per_area)

title: Migration Guide: legacy v0.1.x names to v1.0.0
text: Earlier development versions: default link_phi = "identity", correct choice phi pinned via survey design (n + deff fixed_params$phi), unsafe phi estimated. Current v1.0.0 behaviour: default now NULL resolves automatically: link_phi = "identity" phi pinned (fixed mode). link_phi = "log" otherwise (random / hyperprior mode – brms default). User-supplied "identity" random mode emits clear warning risk divergent transitions.

what: 2b. hbm_betalogitnorm(): phi prior simplified to brms default
title: Migration Guide: legacy v0.1.x names to v1.0.0
text: Earlier development versions: random mode, phi given hierarchical hyperprior: wrapper declared alpha beta Stan parameters auto-injected hyperprior sampling statements via stanvars. Current v1.0.0 behaviour: phi uses brms’s default prior Gamma(0.01,0.01)\mathrm{Gamma}(0.01, 0.01) (lower bound 0). wrapper longer declares alpha beta, longer injects stanvars sampling statements . : prior alpha (declared real<lower=1> Stan) boundary support, routinely producing divergent transitions weakly-informative data. simpler brms default also means prior = NULL exactly users expect. Migration: legacy code still passes stanvars containing sampling statements alpha / beta hbm_betalogitnorm() now raises informative error pointing users new mechanism (prior = brms::set_prior("gamma(...)", class = "phi")). need reproduce exact pre-v1.0.0 hierarchical model (e.g. verify earlier analysis), drop universal hbm_flex() interface, doesn’t apply hbm_betalogitnorm() guard accepts arbitrary user-declared Stan parameters. See vignette("hbsaems-betalogitnorm-model") side--side code listing.
code: phi   ~ gamma(alpha, beta) alpha ~ gamma(1, 1)              (Stan parameter, real<lower=1>) beta  ~ gamma(1, 1)              (Stan parameter, real<lower=0>)

what: 2c. CRITICAL bug fix: sampling_variance no longer silently corrupted
title: Migration Guide: legacy v0.1.x names to v1.0.0
text: Earlier development versions: hbm() sampling_variance = "D" stored Di\sqrt{D_i} hidden offset column attached sigma ~ 0 + offset(.hbsaems_sigma_fixed) formula. However, brms applies dpar’s link function (default link_sigma = "log" Gaussian / Lognormal / Student / etc.) linear predictor plugging likelihood – Stan model actually computed σi=exp⁡(Di)\sigma_i = \exp(\sqrt{D_i}) instead Di\sqrt{D_i}. E.g.Di=4D_i = 4 given σi=2.0\sigma_i = 2.0 produced σi≈7.39\sigma_i \approx 7.39 – catastrophic miscalibration. bug affected fixed_params = list(sigma = ...). Current v1.0.0 behaviour: hbm() now overrides link_<par> = "identity" family object every pinned distributional parameter, offset values passed verbatim. user action required – existing code automatically benefits fix. single important behavioural change v1.0.0; fitting Fay-Herriot models sampling_variance earlier hbsaems development version, estimates biased strongly recommend refitting.

what: 3. sae_scale(): zero-variance predictions no longer corrupt downstream data
title: Migration Guide: legacy v0.1.x names to v1.0.0
text: Earlier development versions: area predictions identical (e.g. toy fit), base::scale() produced NaN throughout, silently corrupting result_table. Current v1.0.0 behaviour: detects case, emits warning, returns centred-(raw) predictions instead NaN.

title: Migration Guide: legacy v0.1.x names to v1.0.0
text: auxiliary covariates estimated survey come known sampling SE, can now declare measurement error without writing brms mi() syntax hand: Internally hbm() rewrites RHS mi(x1, se_x1) + x2 delegating brms. Caveat: measurement-error models inflate parameter space; expect 2-5x longer sampling time consider adapt_delta = 0.99 combining smooth terms.
code: fit <- hbm(   formula = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2),   data    = mydata,   measurement_error = list(x1 = "se_x1"),   # x1 has known SE in se_x1 column   sampling_variance = "D",                  # Fay-Herriot   re      = ~ (1 | regency) )

title: Migration Guide: legacy v0.1.x names to v1.0.0
text: always written mi(...) explicitly formula, hbm() longer requires set handle_missing – call silently routes model-based imputation:
code: # Previously: had to spell out handle_missing hbm(bf(y | mi() ~ mi(x1) + x2) + bf(x1 | mi() ~ z),     data = d, handle_missing = "model")  # v1.0.0: handle_missing inferred from formula hbm(bf(y | mi() ~ mi(x1) + x2) + bf(x1 | mi() ~ z),     data = d)

title: hbsaems — Hierarchical Bayesian Area-Level SAE Models
text: hbsaems R package area-level Hierarchical Bayesian Small Area Estimation (HBSAE). methodological foundation follows standard SAE literature – primarily Rao Molina (2015) – computational implementation adapted parameterisation prior-specification conventions brms package (Bürkner, 2017), targets Stan back-end. package designed support principled Bayesian workflow (Gelman others, 2020) SAE modelling becomes systematic: prior predictive checks, MCMC convergence diagnostics, posterior predictive checks, leave-one-cross-validation, Bayesian model comparison averaging, prior sensitivity analysis, design- consistent benchmarking part standard pipeline. Scope. package focuses area-level SAE models. Unit- level SAE models (e.g. nested error model Battese, Harter & Fuller, 1988) focus.

title: hbsaems — Hierarchical Bayesian Area-Level SAE Models
text: Area-level SAE models: Fay-Herriot normal, lognormal-lognormal, beta logit-normal, binomial logit-normal – adapted Rao Molina (2015) brms parameterisation. SAE-idiomatic argument naming (v1.0.0): arguments named SAE concepts (auxiliary, area_var, spatial_var, spatial_model, sampling_variance, n, deff). Beta precision: ϕi=ni/deffi−1\phi_i = n_i / \mathrm{deff}_i - 1 pinned via offset. Lognormal: σi=ψi\sigma_i = \sqrt{\psi_i} pinned via offset (Fay-Herriot lognormal). Generic fixed_params mechanism power users custom distributions. Spatial random effects: CAR (ICAR / proper / BYM2) SAR (lag / error). Custom brms distributions: built-Loglogistic Shifted Loglogistic, plus registration framework user-defined families. Missing data: listwise deletion, multiple imputation (mice), joint Bayesian imputation (brms::mi()) auto-selection. Comprehensive Bayesian workflow: prior predictive checks, MCMC convergence diagnostics, posterior predictive checks, LOO-CV, Bayesian model comparison averaging, prior sensitivity analysis via priorsense, design-consistent benchmarking. Shrinkage priors: Horseshoe R2D2 selectable via prior_type. Nonlinear smooth terms: thin-plate splines Gaussian processes. Interactive Shiny application: bilingual (English/Indonesian) GUI via run_sae_app(). Ten comprehensive vignettes covering every supported model advanced topic.

title: hbsaems — Hierarchical Bayesian Area-Level SAE Models
code: library(hbsaems)  # Load example data data("data_fhnorm") str(data_fhnorm[, c("regency", "province", "y", "D", "x1", "x2")])  # Fit a basic Fay-Herriot Normal model with an IID area effect model <- hbm(   formula     = bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   hb_sampling = "gaussian",   hb_link     = "identity",   re          = ~(1 | regency),   data        = data_fhnorm,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 2, seed = 1 )  # Check the model summary summary(model)

code: hbm()                       Universal: any brms family, full customisation                                        ▲                                        |                       hbm_flex()                       Family registry + auxiliary + fixed_params                                        ▲                                        |        hbm_lnln() / hbm_betalogitnorm() / hbm_binlogitnorm()        SAE-friendly wrappers: response, auxiliary, area_var, ...

title: hbsaems — Hierarchical Bayesian Area-Level SAE Models
code: model <- hbm(   formula        = bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),  # brms formula   hb_sampling    = "gaussian",            # likelihood family   hb_link        = "identity",            # link function   re             = ~(1 | regency),        # IID area RE (formula)   spatial_var    = "province",            # spatial RE column   spatial_model  = "car",                 # "car" or "sar"   M              = adjacency_matrix_car,  # spatial weight matrix   data           = data_fhnorm )

title: hbsaems — Hierarchical Bayesian Area-Level SAE Models
text: Beta logit-normal proportions (0,1)(0, 1): Binomial logit-normal successes trials: Lognormal-lognormal (Fay-Herriot variant) positive, right-skewed responses:
code: fit_beta <- hbm_betalogitnorm(   response  = "y",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   n         = "n",                # sample size column   deff      = "deff",             # design effect column   area_var  = "regency",          # area random effect   data      = data_betalogitnorm,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 2, seed = 1 ) fit_bin <- hbm_binlogitnorm(   response  = "y",   trials    = "n",   auxiliary = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var  = "district",         # 100 districts in this dataset   data      = data_binlogitnorm,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 2, seed = 1 ) fit_lnln <- hbm_lnln(   response     = "y_obs",   auxiliary    = c("x1", "x2", "x3"),   area_var     = "district",   sampling_variance = "psi_i",          # known sampling variance (log scale)   data         = data_lnln,   chains = 4, iter = 4000, warmup = 2000, cores = 2, seed = 1 )

title: hbsaems — Hierarchical Bayesian Area-Level SAE Models
text: hbsaems ships two custom brms families positive, right- skewed data: Loglogistic – canonical Fisk parameterisation following flexsurv::dllogis Wikipedia. See ?loglogistic ?brms_custom_loglogistic. Shifted Loglogistic – GEV-style parameterisation Hosking & Wallis (1997). See ?shifted_loglogistic ?brms_custom_shifted_loglogistic. auto-registered brms package load via register_hbsae_brms_custom().

what: Build a brms Custom Family + Stanvars Pair from a Single Spec — build_brms_custom_family
title: Build a brms Custom Family + Stanvars Pair from a Single Spec — build_brms_custom_family
text: Convenience function combines custom_family stanvar standard list(custom_family, stanvars_family) pair returned every brms_custom_* helper hbsaems.  Stan code read directly inst/stan/<name>.stan – user need maintain R string.

title: Build a brms Custom Family + Stanvars Pair from a Single Spec — build_brms_custom_family
code: build_brms_custom_family(   name,   dpars,   links,   lb = NA,   ub = NA,   type = c("real", "int"),   loop = FALSE,   log_lik = NULL,   posterior_predict = NULL,   posterior_epred = NULL,   use_stan_native = FALSE )

title: Build a brms Custom Family + Stanvars Pair from a Single Spec — build_brms_custom_family
text: name Character.  Family name; must match <name>.stan file inst/stan/. dpars Character vector distributional parameter names.  first MUST "mu" (brms convention). links Character vector link functions, length dpars.  Common values: "identity", "log", "logit". lb, ub Numeric vectors lower / upper bounds; NA none. type "real" (continuous) "int" (discrete). loop Logical.  FALSE (default) selects vectorised brms convention (Stan signatures take vectors y mu); TRUE selects scalar Stan signatures.  default matches neodistr convention.  Whichever choose, corresponding .stan file inst/stan/ must use convention. log_lik Optional function computing observation-level log-likelihoods (used loo(), waic()).  Signature must function(, prep).  See brms vignette "Define Custom Response Distributions" details. posterior_predict Optional function drawing posterior predictive distribution (used pp_check(), posterior_predict()).  Signature function(, prep, ...). posterior_epred Optional function returning conditional expectation \(E[Y \mid X]\) (used fitted(), conditional_effects()).  Signature function(prep). use_stan_native Logical.  TRUE, stanvars block attached – <name>_lpdf function assumed exist Stan built-(e.g.\ loglogistic_lpdf Stan \(\geq\) 2.29).  FALSE (default), function definition loaded inst/stan/<name>.stan.

title: Build a brms Custom Family + Stanvars Pair from a Single Spec — build_brms_custom_family
text: list two elements: custom_family brms::customfamily object. stanvars_family brms::stanvars object     Stan code functions block, NULL     use_stan_native = TRUE.

title: Build a brms Custom Family + Stanvars Pair from a Single Spec — build_brms_custom_family
text: Stan code built per call; code reused many times, wrap returned object function call lazily.

title: Build a brms Custom Family + Stanvars Pair from a Single Spec — build_brms_custom_family
code: # \donttest{ library(hbsaems) library(brms)  # Build the loglogistic family.  Stan code is loaded from # inst/stan/hbsae_loglogistic.stan — the `hbsae_` prefix avoids # collision with Stan's BUILT-IN `loglogistic_lpdf` (Stan >= 2.29). ll <- build_brms_custom_family(   name             = "hbsae_loglogistic",   dpars            = c("mu", "beta"),   links            = c("log", "log"),   lb               = c(0,    0),   ub               = c(NA,   NA),   type             = "real" ) class(ll$custom_family) #> [1] "customfamily" "brmsfamily"   "family"       # }

what: build_spatial_weight: Construct M for CAR / SAR models — build_spatial_weight
title: build_spatial_weight: Construct M for CAR / SAR models — build_spatial_weight
text: Constructs spatial weight / adjacency matrix \(M\) suitable use M argument hbm.  function accepts either path shapefile (.shp) sf / Spatial* object already memory, returns square numeric matrix automatically validated theoretical requirements target model class via check_spatial_weight.

title: build_spatial_weight: Construct M for CAR / SAR models — build_spatial_weight
code: build_spatial_weight(   shp,   for_model = NULL,   type = NULL,   style = NULL,   k = 4L,   threshold = NULL,   id_col = NULL,   longlat = FALSE,   validate = TRUE )

title: build_spatial_weight: Construct M for CAR / SAR models — build_spatial_weight
text: shp Either character path .shp file, sf object, Spatial* (sp) object. for_model Optional convenience argument: "car" "sar".  supplied, sets sensible defaults type style documented .  user also supplies type style explicitly, take precedence. type Character.  Neighbour definition: "queen" Polygons sharing least one vertex.     Symmetric. "rook" Polygons sharing edge.  Symmetric. "knn" k nearest centroids.  Asymmetric     general; auto-symmetrised style = "B". "distance" Centroids within threshold.     Symmetric. style Character.  "B" (binary, suitable CAR) "W" (row-standardised, suitable SAR). k Integer.  Number nearest neighbours type = "knn" (default 4). threshold Numeric.  Distance threshold (CRS units) type = "distance".  NULL, maximum first-nearest-neighbour distance used. id_col Optional character.  Column name spatial object whose values become matrix dimnames. longlat Logical.  Whether coordinates longitude/latitude. validate Logical.  Whether run check_spatial_weight construction (default TRUE).  Set FALSE skip diagnostics.

title: build_spatial_weight: Construct M for CAR / SAR models — build_spatial_weight
text: square numeric matrix row/column names taken   id_col (supplied) sequential integers, plus   following attributes: "hbsaems_type", "hbsaems_style",   "hbsaems_for_model", "hbsaems_check" (result   check_spatial_weight).
