title: Compare One or Two Fitted HBMs — model_compare
code: # \donttest{ library(hbsaems) library(brms) data("data_fhnorm") FAST <- list(chains = 4, iter = 2000, warmup = 1000, cores = 1,              seed = 123, refresh = 0)  m1 <- do.call(hbm, c(list(formula = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),                           data = data_fhnorm), FAST)) #> Warning: Model fitted without any area-level random effects. #>   This is unusual for Small Area Estimation: the standard Fay-Herriot model assumes u_i ~ N(0, sigma_u^2) per area, so estimates from a purely fixed-effects model will not borrow strength across areas. #>   Consider one of: #>     re = ~ (1 | area_id)                                     # IID area RE #>     spatial_var = 'area_id', spatial_model = 'car', M = W    # CAR spatial RE #>     spatial_var = 'area_id', spatial_model = 'sar', M = W    # SAR spatial RE #>   If a fixed-effects-only baseline is intentional, you can suppress this warning with `suppressWarnings()`. #> Compiling Stan program... #> Start sampling m2 <- do.call(hbm, c(list(formula = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2),                           data = data_fhnorm), FAST)) #> Warning: Model fitted without any area-level random effects. #>   This is unusual for Small Area Estimation: the standard Fay-Herriot model assumes u_i ~ N(0, sigma_u^2) per area, so estimates from a purely fixed-effects model will not borrow strength across areas. #>   Consider one of: #>     re = ~ (1 | area_id)                                     # IID area RE #>     spatial_var = 'area_id', spatial_model = 'car', M = W    # CAR spatial RE #>     spatial_var = 'area_id', spatial_model = 'sar', M = W    # SAR spatial RE #>   If a fixed-effects-only baseline is intentional, you can suppress this warning with `suppressWarnings()`. #> Compiling Stan program... #> Start sampling  model_compare(m1)            # single-model goodness-of-fit #> Warning:  #> 1 (1.0%) p_waic estimates greater than 0.4. We recommend trying loo instead. #>  #> Model Comparison  [hbmc_results] #> ----------------------------------- #>  ELPD-LOO  (m1): -179.31 #>  ELPD-WAIC (m1): -179.27 #>  model_compare(m1, m2)        # pairwise comparison #> Warning:  #> 1 (1.0%) p_waic estimates greater than 0.4. We recommend trying loo instead. #> Warning:  #> 1 (1.0%) p_waic estimates greater than 0.4. We recommend trying loo instead. #> Iteration: 1 #> Iteration: 2 #> Iteration: 3 #> Iteration: 4 #> Iteration: 5 #> Iteration: 1 #> Iteration: 2 #> Iteration: 3 #> Iteration: 4 #> Iteration: 5 #>  #> Model Comparison  [hbmc_results] #> ----------------------------------- #>  ELPD-LOO  (m1): -179.31 #>  ELPD-LOO  (m2): -180.94 #>  ELPD-WAIC (m1): -179.27 #>  ELPD-WAIC (m2): -180.92 #>  # }

what: Prior Predictive Check for Fitted HBMs — prior_check
title: Prior Predictive Check for Fitted HBMs — prior_check

code: prior_check(model, data = NULL, response_var = NULL, ndraws_ppc = 50, ...)

title: Prior Predictive Check for Fitted HBMs — prior_check
text: model hbmfit brmsfit object fit sample_prior = "" (see hbm). data data.frame containing response variable. Optional since 1.1.0: NULL (default) data frame stored fitted model used. response_var Character scalar naming response column. Optional since 1.1.0: NULL (default) determined model formula's left-hand side. ndraws_ppc Integer.  Number prior predictive draws overlay plot (default 50). ... Currently unused; reserved future extensions.

title: Prior Predictive Check for Fitted HBMs — prior_check
text: hbpc_results object components: prior_predictive_plot ggplot       pp_check, NULL       generated. prior_draws draws matrix       posterior_predict sized       ndraws_ppc \times nrow(data). observed observed response vector.

title: Prior Predictive Check for Fitted HBMs — prior_check
text: prior predictive distribution $$p(y_{\text{rep}}) =   \int p(y_{\text{rep}} \mid \theta)\, p(\theta) \, \mathrm{d}\theta,$$ marginal distribution new data prior alone.  Comparing observed data fast sanity check: prior predictive places mass anywhere near data, priors likely tight wrong location.

title: Prior Predictive Check for Fitted HBMs — prior_check
text: data omitted taken model$data (model frame stored fit).  response_var omitted read model formula via brmsterms; formula left-hand side (response can determined), error raised asking caller supply response_var explicitly.

title: Prior Predictive Check for Fitted HBMs — prior_check
code: # \donttest{ library(hbsaems) library(brms) data("data_fhnorm") model_prior <- hbm(   formula      = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   data         = data_fhnorm,   sample_prior = "only",   prior        = c(     brms::prior(normal(0, 1), class = "b"),     brms::prior(normal(0, 5), class = "Intercept")   ),   chains = 4, iter = 2000, warmup = 1000, cores = 1,   seed = 42, refresh = 0 ) #> Warning: Model fitted without any area-level random effects. #>   This is unusual for Small Area Estimation: the standard Fay-Herriot model assumes u_i ~ N(0, sigma_u^2) per area, so estimates from a purely fixed-effects model will not borrow strength across areas. #>   Consider one of: #>     re = ~ (1 | area_id)                                     # IID area RE #>     spatial_var = 'area_id', spatial_model = 'car', M = W    # CAR spatial RE #>     spatial_var = 'area_id', spatial_model = 'sar', M = W    # SAR spatial RE #>   If a fixed-effects-only baseline is intentional, you can suppress this warning with `suppressWarnings()`. #> Compiling Stan program... #> Start sampling  # Explicit (as before): pc <- prior_check(model_prior, data = data_fhnorm, response_var = "y")  # New in 1.1.0 -- data and response auto-detected from the model: pc <- prior_check(model_prior) print(pc) #>  #> Prior Predictive Check  [hbpc_results] #> ---------------------------------------- #>  Prior draws  : 50 x 100  #>  Observations : 100  #>  plot(pc)  # }

what: Power-Scale Prior Sensitivity Diagnostics for Fitted HBMs — prior_sensitivity
title: Power-Scale Prior Sensitivity Diagnostics for Fitted HBMs — prior_sensitivity
text: Computes prior likelihood power-scaling sensitivity diagnostics fitted hbmfit model using priorsense package. Useful assessing whether posterior conclusions driven prior data – critical step principled Bayesian SAE workflow.

title: Power-Scale Prior Sensitivity Diagnostics for Fitted HBMs — prior_sensitivity
code: prior_sensitivity(model, ...)

title: Power-Scale Prior Sensitivity Diagnostics for Fitted HBMs — prior_sensitivity
text: model hbmfit object returned hbm (one wrappers) brmsfit object directly. ... Additional arguments forwarded priorsense::powerscale_sensitivity(), e.g.\ variable = c("b_x1", "sd_regency__Intercept") restrict report specific parameters.

title: Power-Scale Prior Sensitivity Diagnostics for Fitted HBMs — prior_sensitivity
text: powerscale_sensitivity_summary object (data frame)   one row per monitored parameter columns   variable, prior, likelihood, diagnosis.   NULL (message) priorsense package   installed.

title: Power-Scale Prior Sensitivity Diagnostics for Fitted HBMs — prior_sensitivity
text: Prior sensitivity analysis answers question: “used slightly different prior, substantive conclusions change?”. power-scaling approach Kallioinen et al.\ (2023) detects: Prior–likelihood conflict: posterior moves     non-negligibly prior - -weighted.  Often     indicates overly informative misspecified prior. Weak likelihood: posterior dominated     prior.  Common SAE areas sampled units. Reported diagnostics include Kullback–Leibler divergence original posterior power-scaled posterior (prior, likelihood) categorical flag (prior-data conflict, strong prior, -). Computational cost.  re-sampling required: importance sampling reuses existing posterior draws.  Hence typical run costs seconds even large hierarchical models.

title: Power-Scale Prior Sensitivity Diagnostics for Fitted HBMs — prior_sensitivity
text: Always.  Specifically: every model fit, drawing substantive conclusions. Whenever convergence diagnostics         convergence_check() clean posterior         seems implausibly narrow implausibly wide. comparing models shrinkage priors – horseshoe         R2D2 informative, small differences         hyperparameters can move estimates noticeably.

title: Power-Scale Prior Sensitivity Diagnostics for Fitted HBMs — prior_sensitivity
text: Kallioinen, N., Paananen, T., Burkner, P.-C., & Vehtari, .\ (2024). Detecting diagnosing prior likelihood sensitivity power-scaling.  Statistics Computing, 34, 57. doi:10.1007/s11222-023-10366-5

what: Read the Stan Function Code for a Custom Distribution — read_stan_function
title: Read the Stan Function Code for a Custom Distribution — read_stan_function
text: Loads contents inst/stan/<name>.stan installed hbsaems (local source tree running tests).  thin wrapper around readLines() resolves package path validates file exists.

title: Read the Stan Function Code for a Custom Distribution — read_stan_function
code: read_stan_function(name)

title: Read the Stan Function Code for a Custom Distribution — read_stan_function
text: name Character.  distribution name – must match <name>.stan file shipped inst/stan/.

title: Read the Stan Function Code for a Custom Distribution — read_stan_function
text: character scalar containing Stan code (functions block).

title: Read the Stan Function Code for a Custom Distribution — read_stan_function
code: code <- read_stan_function("hbsae_loglogistic") cat(code) #> // ============================================================================= #> // inst/stan/loglogistic.stan #> // #> // Stan function definitions for the Loglogistic (Fisk) distribution. #> // #> // IMPORTANT NAMING CONVENTION #> // --------------------------- #> // Since Stan 2.29, the language provides a built-in function called #> //   `loglogistic_lpdf(y | alpha, beta)` #> // (see https://mc-stan.org/docs/functions-reference/log-logistic-distribution.html). #> // #> // brms's custom_family() will generate a target += loglogistic_lpdf(...) #> // call from the family name we pass to brms::custom_family().  If we #> // reuse the bare name `loglogistic` for our custom family, Stan would #> // see TWO functions with the same signature (our user-defined one and #> // the built-in) and refuse to compile -- an unrecoverable collision. #> // #> // Solution: register the custom family under the name "hbsae_loglogistic" #> // (defined in R/dist-loglogistic.R via brms_custom_loglogistic()), #> // so brms generates target += hbsae_loglogistic_lpdf(...) and there is #> // no ambiguity with the Stan built-in.  The user-facing R helpers #> // dloglogistic(), ploglogistic(), qloglogistic(), rloglogistic() KEEP #> // their natural names since they live in the R namespace, not the #> // Stan namespace. #> // #> // Parameterisation #> // ---------------- #> //   Y ~ Loglogistic(mu, beta) with mu > 0 (scale = median) and #> //   beta > 0 (shape).  This matches the Fisk parameterisation used by #> //   flexsurv and eha, and matches Stan native loglogistic_lpdf with #> //   alpha replaced by mu. #> // #> //   PDF:  f(y) = (beta / mu) * (y / mu)^(beta - 1) / [1 + (y / mu)^beta]^2 #> //   CDF:  F(y) = 1 / [1 + (y / mu)^(-beta)] #> // #> // Conventions #> // ----------- #> //   * VECTORISED signatures (neodistr convention: loop = FALSE in #> //     custom_family).  lpdf / lcdf / lccdf take vectors of y and mu and #> //     return a single accumulated log-density / log-CDF / log-CCDF. #> //   * rng remains scalar (one draw per call) since brms post-processing #> //     does the per-observation looping in R. #> //   * Function names match brms's required pattern: #> //       hbsae_loglogistic_lpdf  /  hbsae_loglogistic_lcdf  / #> //       hbsae_loglogistic_lccdf /  hbsae_loglogistic_rng #> // #> // References #> // ---------- #> //   Bennett, S. (1983). Log-logistic regression models for survival #> //     data. JRSS-C, 32(2), 165-171.  <doi:10.2307/2347295> #> //   Kleiber, C., & Kotz, S. (2003). Statistical Size Distributions in #> //     Economics and Actuarial Sciences. Wiley. #> // ============================================================================= #>  #> real hbsae_loglogistic_lpdf(vector y, vector mu, real beta) { #>   int N = num_elements(y); #>   vector[N] z = y ./ mu; #>   // sum_i [ log(beta) - log(mu_i) + (beta - 1) log(z_i) - 2 log1p(z_i^beta) ] #>   return  N * log(beta) #>           - sum(log(mu)) #>           + (beta - 1) * sum(log(z)) #>           - 2 * sum(log1p(pow(z, beta))); #> } #>  #> real hbsae_loglogistic_lcdf(vector y, vector mu, real beta) { #>   int N = num_elements(y); #>   vector[N] z = y ./ mu; #>   // log F(y_i) = -log1p(z_i^(-beta));  return sum over i. #>   return -sum(log1p(pow(z, -beta))); #> } #>  #> real hbsae_loglogistic_lccdf(vector y, vector mu, real beta) { #>   int N = num_elements(y); #>   vector[N] z = y ./ mu; #>   // log(1 - F(y_i)) = -log1p(z_i^beta);  return sum over i. #>   return -sum(log1p(pow(z, beta))); #> } #>  #> real hbsae_loglogistic_rng(real mu, real beta) { #>   // Inverse CDF method: q = mu * (u / (1 - u))^(1 / beta) #>   real u = uniform_rng(0, 1); #>   return mu * pow(u / (1 - u), 1.0 / beta); #> }

Typically produced brms::custom_family() helper brms_custom_loglogistic. stanvars brms::stanvars object containing Stan-side function definitions (log-PDF / log-CDF / RNG). response_check Optional function function(y) -> logical response-domain validation (e.g.\ positivity check loglogistic). response_check_msg Character.  Error message response_check fails. supports_mi Logical.  Whether brms::mi() can impute response likelihood (default FALSE; custom families typically support mi()). discrete Logical.  Whether family discrete (default FALSE). overwrite Logical.  TRUE, replace existing entry key.

title: Register a brms Custom Family with the hbsaems Model Registry — register_hbsae_brms_custom
text: registration, family usable following ways: Internally, hbm detects registered family brms::custom_family () passes family object directly brms::brm() instead constructing built-brms::brmsfamily(), (ii) merges family's stanvars user-supplied stanvars.
code: # Direct via the registry key   fit <- hbm_flex("loglogistic", response = "y",                    auxiliary = c("x1", "x2"),                    data = d, area_var = "area")    # Direct via hbm() (canonical):   fit <- hbm(brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + (1 | area)),              data = d,              hb_sampling = "loglogistic")

title: Register a brms Custom Family with the hbsaems Model Registry — register_hbsae_brms_custom
code: # \donttest{ library(hbsaems) library(brms)  # Loglogistic (built in to hbsaems 1.0.0; this just shows the # registration mechanism).  We use a key with a "_user" suffix to # avoid shadowing the built-in "loglogistic" entry, and unregister # at the end so re-running this example block keeps the registry # clean. ll <- brms_custom_loglogistic() if ("loglogistic_user" %in% list_hbsae_models()) {   # Idempotent rerun: remove any previous registration first.   rm("loglogistic_user", envir = hbsaems:::.hbsae_model_env) } register_hbsae_brms_custom(   key             = "loglogistic_user",   custom_family   = ll$custom_family,   stanvars        = ll$stanvars_family,   response_check  = function(y) all(y > 0, na.rm = TRUE),   response_check_msg = "Loglogistic response must be positive." ) "loglogistic_user" %in% list_hbsae_models() #> [1] TRUE  # Cleanup so the registry is unchanged after the example runs. rm("loglogistic_user", envir = hbsaems:::.hbsae_model_env) # }

NULL,   aux_param_hyperprior = NULL,   overwrite = FALSE )

title: Register a Custom HBSAE Model — register_hbsae_model
text: key Character.  Unique identifier (e.g.\ "gamma_log"). family Character.  brms family name passed hb_sampling. link Character.  Default link function (default "identity"). discrete Logical.  response discrete?  Affects whether handle_missing = "model" (joint Bayesian imputation via brms::mi()) allowed (default FALSE). supports_mi Logical.  Whether brms::mi() can impute response variable family (default !discrete). has_addition_term Logical.  Whether LHS uses addition term |\ trials(n) (default FALSE). addition_template Character.  sprintf template used has_addition_term = TRUE.  Must contain three %s slots response, addition variable, RHS.  Example: "%s | trials(%s) ~ %s". response_check Function function(y) returning TRUE response domain valid, FALSE otherwise (default: accept anything). response_check_msg Character.  Error message displayed response_check(y) returns FALSE. default_priors Function function(...) returning brmsprior object, NULL use brms defaults. aux_param_hyperprior Optional function function(args, data) returning list components prior (brmsprior) stanvars (stanvar object).  Used distributions auxiliary parameter (e.g.\ \(\phi\) Beta, shape Gamma) requiring hyperprior expressed raw Stan code.  args list contains family-specific user inputs forwarded aux_args hbm_flex.  Return NULL skip injection given call. overwrite Logical.  Permit overwriting existing key (default FALSE).

what: Benchmark Small-Area Estimates to Known Totals — sae_benchmark
title: Benchmark Small-Area Estimates to Known Totals — sae_benchmark
text: Adjusts area-level predictions weighted sum matches one known aggregate “benchmark” totals (e.g.\ official provincial national figures).  Two modes supported:

title: Benchmark Small-Area Estimates to Known Totals — sae_benchmark
code: sae_benchmark(   predictions,   target,   weights = NULL,   target_type = c("total", "mean"),   method = c("ratio", "difference", "raking"),   groups = NULL,   posterior = NULL,   probs = c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975),   max_iter = 100L,   tol = 1e-08 )

title: Benchmark Small-Area Estimates to Known Totals — sae_benchmark
text: predictions hbsae_results object produced sae_predict. target Numeric.  method = "ratio" "difference": single benchmark total \(T\).  method = "raking": numeric vector group totals (one per group; see groups). weights Optional numeric vector length equal number areas predictions.  Official-Statistics practice normally population size \(N_i\) area, \(\sum_i w_i \hat{\theta}_i\) implied population total.  NULL, safe default chosen based target_type; informational message emitted users can see exactly weighting applied. target_type Character.  Either "total" (default) "mean".  Consulted weights = NULL choose safe default: "total" sets weights = rep(1, n) \(\sum_i w_i \hat{\theta}_i = \sum_i \hat{\theta}_i\); "mean" sets weights = rep(1/n, n) weighted sum equals mean.  Ignored weights supplied.  Always prefer pass explicit weights (typically population size \(N_i\)) production code: default heuristic provided avoid silent scale corruption user forgets argument. method Character.  One : "ratio" Multiplicative:     \(\hat{\theta}_i^B = \hat{\theta}_i \times T /       \sum_j w_j \hat{\theta}_j\). "difference" Additive:     \(\hat{\theta}_i^B = \hat{\theta}_i +       (T - \sum_j w_j \hat{\theta}_j) / \sum_j w_j\). "raking" Iterative proportional fitting multiple     group totals.  Requires groups. groups Optional integer/character vector length equal number areas, assigning area benchmarking group. Required method = "raking"; ignored otherwise. posterior Optional logical matrix.  Controls Bayesian mode: NULL FALSE (default) Point-estimate mode –     behaviour v1.0.0. TRUE Bayesian mode.  Posterior draws extracted     predictions automatically (requires     predictions$model available). numeric matrix (\(D \times n\)) Bayesian mode     user-supplied draws (\(D\) draws, \(n\) areas). probs Numeric vector quantile probabilities summarise adjusted posterior (default c(0.025, 0.5, 0.975)). used Bayesian mode. max_iter Integer.  Maximum iterations raking (default 100L). tol Numeric.  Convergence tolerance raking (default 1e-8).

title: Benchmark Small-Area Estimates to Known Totals — sae_benchmark
text: hbsae_results object benchmarked   Prediction values, plus additional element   $benchmark_info records method, target,   weights, implied adjustment factor,   converged (logical, raking ).  Bayesian mode   result_table also contains updated SD   RSE_percent columns reflecting post-benchmark posterior   uncertainty, plus quantile columns named probs.

title: Benchmark Small-Area Estimates to Known Totals — sae_benchmark
text: Point-estimate mode (default) Applies adjustment     posterior mean.  RSE column left unchanged     working approximation. Fully Bayesian mode (posterior = TRUE supply     posterior matrix) Applies adjustment every     posterior draw recomputes SD, quantiles, RSE     adjusted draws.  statistically correct procedure     produces proper uncertainty intervals benchmarking.

title: Benchmark Small-Area Estimates to Known Totals — sae_benchmark
text: Benchmarking widely used official statistics ensure   consistency model-based small-area estimates published   aggregate totals reliable source.

title: Benchmark Small-Area Estimates to Known Totals — sae_benchmark
text: Applying deterministic adjustment factor posterior point   estimate distorts uncertainty structure: multiplicative   factor scales mean SD amount (  RSE percentage preserved), additive shift   change SD, RSE percentage shrinks small areas   grows large ones.  Neither post-hoc fixes   guaranteed correct general.  Bayesian mode every draw   benchmarked independently, resulting posterior carries   right uncertainty.

title: Benchmark Small-Area Estimates to Known Totals — sae_benchmark
text: Pfeffermann, D. (2013).  New important developments small area estimation.  Statistical Science 28(1), 40–68. Wang, J., Fuller, W. ., & Qu, Y. (2008).  Small area estimation restriction.  Survey Methodology 34, 29–36.

title: Benchmark Small-Area Estimates to Known Totals — sae_benchmark
code: # Synthetic predictions (point-estimate mode) p <- structure(   list(     result_table = data.frame(Prediction  = c(10, 12, 9, 11),                                SD          = c(1, 1, 1, 1),                                RSE_percent = c(10, 8, 11, 9)),     rse_model    = 9.5,     pred         = c(10, 12, 9, 11)   ),   class = "hbsae_results" ) bm1 <- sae_benchmark(p, target = 50, method = "ratio") #> Using default weights for `target_type = "total"`. #>   For production use, pass `weights = <population size per area>` to be explicit.  # Fully Bayesian mode with user-supplied draws set.seed(1) D <- 1000 draws <- matrix(rnorm(D * 4, mean = c(10, 12, 9, 11), sd = 1),                  nrow = D, byrow = TRUE) bm2 <- sae_benchmark(p, target = 50, method = "ratio",                       posterior = draws) #> Using default weights for `target_type = "total"`. #>   For production use, pass `weights = <population size per area>` to be explicit. bm2$result_table              # SD, RSE updated from draws #>   Prediction       SD RSE_percent      Q2.5      Q50    Q97.5 #> 1   11.90476 1.075221    9.031856  9.636304 11.83747 14.01357 #> 2   14.28571 1.078090    7.546629 12.155259 14.29365 16.38833 #> 3   10.71429 1.099963   10.266317  8.589336 10.68974 12.98409 #> 4   13.09524 1.057279    8.073767 10.917463 13.07240 15.16273

title: Generate Small Area Estimates — sae_predict
text: Primary SAE prediction function hbsaems (supersedes deprecated hbsae).  Computes area-level posterior predictive means, standard deviations, relative standard errors (RSE).

title: Generate Small Area Estimates — sae_predict
code: sae_predict(   model,   newdata = NULL,   predict_type = c("epred", "response", "linpred", "proportion"),   ... )

title: Generate Small Area Estimates — sae_predict
text: model hbmfit brmsfit object. newdata Optional new data.frame prediction unsampled areas.  NULL (default), original data used. predict_type Character; posterior quantity summarise.   One : "epred" (default, new 1.1.0) Posterior area       mean \(\theta_i = E[y_i \mid x_i, u_i]\) via       posterior_epred.  correct SAE       target; per-area SD excludes observation-level likelihood       variance. "response" Posterior predictive NEW observation       \(\tilde y_i\) via posterior_predict.        1.0.x behaviour; use predicting fresh observations       aggregate counts observation variability wanted. "linpred" Linear predictor response scale via       posterior_linpred transform = TRUE.       binomial family area proportion \(p_i\). "proportion" (new 1.1.0) area proportion       \(p_i\).  binomial family divides expected count       trials (\(E[y_i]/n_i\)), giving quantity comparable across       areas different sample sizes; identical "linpred".       non-binomial families equals "epred". Binomial note. binomial family posterior_epred()   returns expected count \(n_i p_i\), comparable   across areas unequal \(n_i\).  SAE target normally   proportion \(p_i\), predict_type = "epred" binomial   model automatically returns \(p_i\) (warning); use   "response" expected count, "proportion"   request proportion explicitly. ... Additional arguments forwarded posterior_predict (e.g.\ ndraws, re_formula).

title: Generate Small Area Estimates — sae_predict
text: hbsae_results object components: result_table data.frame columns       Prediction, SD, RSE_percent. rse_model Mean RSE_percent across areas. pred Numeric vector point predictions (= result_table$Prediction).

title: Generate Small Area Estimates — sae_predict
text: area \(= 1, \ldots, n\), function computes $$\widehat{y}_i = \frac{1}{S} \sum_{s=1}^{S} y_{}^{(s)}, \qquad       \widehat{\mathrm{sd}}_i^2 =       \frac{1}{S - 1} \sum_{s=1}^{S}       \left( y_{}^{(s)} - \widehat{y}_i \right)^2,$$ \(\theta_{}^{(s)}\) posterior draws area-mean target (predict_type = "epred", default) – new observation \(y_i^{(s)}\) predict_type = "response" – \(S\) number draws.  relative standard error \(\mathrm{RSE}_i = 100 \cdot |\widehat{\mathrm{sd}}_i / \widehat{y}_i|\).

title: Generate Small Area Estimates — sae_predict
code: # \donttest{ library(hbsaems) library(brms) data("data_fhnorm") model <- hbm(   formula = brms::bf(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3),   data    = data_fhnorm,   chains = 4, iter = 2000, warmup = 1000, cores = 1,   seed = 123, refresh = 0 ) #> Warning: Model fitted without any area-level random effects. #>   This is unusual for Small Area Estimation: the standard Fay-Herriot model assumes u_i ~ N(0, sigma_u^2) per area, so estimates from a purely fixed-effects model will not borrow strength across areas. #>   Consider one of: #>     re = ~ (1 | area_id)                                     # IID area RE #>     spatial_var = 'area_id', spatial_model = 'car', M = W    # CAR spatial RE #>     spatial_var = 'area_id', spatial_model = 'sar', M = W    # SAR spatial RE #>   If a fixed-effects-only baseline is intentional, you can suppress this warning with `suppressWarnings()`. #> Compiling Stan program... #> Start sampling est <- sae_predict(model) summary(est) #>  #> ===== Small Area Estimation Summary ===== #>  #> Areas       : 100  #> Overall RSE : 2.85 % #>  #> Predictions: #>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.  #>   6.879   9.348   9.862   9.892  10.483  13.350  #>  #> RSE by area: #>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.  #>   1.541   2.176   2.646   2.851   3.375   6.286  plot(est, type = "predictions")  plot(est, type = "uncertainty")  # }

title: Shifted (3-Parameter) Loglogistic Distribution — shifted_loglogistic
text: x, q Numeric vector quantiles. mu Location parameter (real; equals median). sigma Scale parameter (sigma > 0). xi Shape parameter (real; xi = 0 gives logistic distribution). log, log.p Logical.  See Distributions. lower.tail Logical.  See Distributions. p Vector probabilities. n Number random draws.

title: Shifted (3-Parameter) Loglogistic Distribution — shifted_loglogistic
text: Numeric vector.

title: Shifted (3-Parameter) Loglogistic Distribution — shifted_loglogistic
text: implementation uses GEV-style parameterisation Hosking & Wallis (1997) Flood Estimation Handbook (Robson & Reed 1999), \(\mu\) pure location parameter (median), \(\sigma\) pure scale parameter \(\xi\) pure shape parameter: $$F(x \mid \mu, \sigma, \xi) =   \{1 + (1 + \xi z)^{-1/\xi}\}^{-1},   \qquad z = (x - \mu) / \sigma,$$ corresponding density $$f(x \mid \mu, \sigma, \xi) =   \frac{(1 + \xi z)^{-(1/\xi + 1)}}        {\sigma \{1 + (1 + \xi z)^{-1/\xi}\}^{2}}.$$ support depends \(\xi\): \(\xi > 0\): \(x \ge \mu - \sigma/\xi\) (bounded ). \(\xi < 0\): \(x \le \mu - \sigma/\xi\) (bounded ). \(\xi = 0\): \(x \\mathbb{R}\) (logistic limit). median always \(\mu\); mean exists \(|\xi| < 1\) \(\mu + \sigma (\alpha\csc\alpha - 1)/\xi\), \(\alpha = \pi\xi\).  Reducing , family contains: standard log-logistic \(\xi = 1\) (reparameterised); logistic distribution \(\xi \0\); generalised Pareto family \(\xi = -1\). parameterisation?  alternative "simple-shift" form, \(Y - \delta \sim \mathrm{LogLogistic}\), exists literature (Geskus 2001) closer spirit brms::shifted_lognormal()'s positive shift ndt.  deliberately follow GEV-style parameterisation provides smooth limit logistic         distribution \(\xi = 0\); parameters \((\mu, \sigma, \xi)\)         orthogonally interpretable (location / scale / shape); canonical form hydrology extreme-value         applications (Hosking & Wallis 1997).

title: Shifted (3-Parameter) Loglogistic Distribution — shifted_loglogistic
text: Geskus, R. B. (2001). Methods estimating AIDS incubation time distribution date seroconversion censored. Statistics Medicine, 20(5), 795-812. Hosking, J. R. M., & Wallis, J. R. (1997). Regional Frequency Analysis: Approach Based L-Moments.  Cambridge University Press.  ISBN 0-521-43045-3. Robson, ., & Reed, D. (1999). Flood Estimation Handbook, Volume 3: Statistical Procedures Flood Frequency Estimation.  Institute Hydrology, Wallingford, UK.

See #> https://mc-stan.org/misc/warnings.html#bfmi-low #> Warning: Examine the pairs() plot to diagnose sampling problems #> Warning: The largest R-hat is 1.44, indicating chains have not mixed. #> Running the chains for more iterations may help. title: hbsaems 1.1.0
text: Minor release Bayesian-workflow improvements. Read behaviour change upgrading production scripts.

title: hbsaems 1.1.0
text: sae_predict() now defaults predict_type = "epred" (posterior distribution area mean theta_i), replacing previous posterior-predictive---new-observation behaviour. theoretically correct target small area estimation: reported SD / RSE_percent now measure uncertainty estimator theta_i therefore smaller 1.0.x (included observation-level likelihood variance). Every RSE number scripts produce change. reproduce old numbers exactly, pass predict_type = "response". third option, "linpred", returns linear predictor response scale.

title: hbsaems 1.1.0
text: Four SAE-oriented families added model registry, usable via hbm_flex() / hbm() list_hbsae_models(): gamma (positive continuous, log link – incomes/expenditures), skew_normal (skewed continuous area means), student (heavy-tailed, robust Fay-Herriot), hurdle_lognormal (positive continuous excess zeros). carries appropriate response-domain check (e.g. Gamma requires y > 0; hurdle-lognormal allows y >= 0). hbm_flex()’s family argument now primary way choose family. accepts string (e.g. "gaussian", exactly like old family_key), brms family object (e.g. gaussian(), binomial()), registered custom family object (e.g. brms_custom_loglogistic()$custom_family). family name matched registered spec, custom distributions reuse Stan code (stanvars); unregistered custom family raises informative error. family_key argument retained backward-compatible alias (deprecated). Supply either family family_key, . hbm() gains family argument mirroring hbm_flex(): pass string (e.g. "gaussian") brms family object (e.g. gaussian(), Gamma(link = "log")); link carried object honoured hb_link left default. hb_sampling retained backward-compatible alias. Supply either family hb_sampling, . family thus uniform distribution selector across hbm() hbm_flex(). sae_predict() gains predict_type = c("epred", "response", "linpred", "proportion") argument (see ). model_average() sae_benchmark() honour . binomial family, posterior_epred() returns expected COUNT (n_i * p_i), comparable across areas unequal trial counts; predict_type = "proportion" returns area proportion p_i, predict_type = "epred" binomial model now auto-converts p_i (warning). Use "response" count. convergence_check() now reports divergent transitions, E-BFMI per chain, max-treedepth hit rate numbers summary() (just energy plot), warns divergences occur E-BFMI < 0.3. model_compare() / model_compare_all() now check Pareto-k PSIS-LOO reliability: n_high_k column added ranking table warning issued k > 0.7. previously dead reloo_args argument now functional – supplying triggers brms::reloo() refit high-k folds.

title: hbsaems 1.1.0
text: hbm() now defaults control = list(adapt_delta = 0.95, max_treedepth = 12) (values long documented recommended starting point) instead brms’s adapt_delta = 0.8. Hierarchical SAE funnels need higher value avoid spurious divergences. User-supplied control values always respected. Seed-level results may differ slightly 1.0.x.

title: hbsaems 1.1.0
text: convergence_check() longer reports Tail_ESS = Bulk_ESS fallback path ‘posterior’ package unavailable; reports NA rather falsely implying tails checked. model_compare() (deprecated hbmc()) accept list fitted models . model_compare() became S3 generic 1.0.0 dispatched hbmfit/brmsfit, pre-1.0.0 convention hbmc(model = list(m1, m2)) failed “applicable method ‘model_compare’ applied object class ‘list’”. model_compare.list() method now unwraps 1-2 element list single/pairwise path (hbmc_results), routes 3+ element list model_compare_all() (ranked hbm_table), rejects empty non-model list informative error.

title: hbsaems 1.0.0
text: CRAN release: 2026-05-25 First stable release. hbsaems fits area-level Hierarchical Bayesian Small Area Estimation models following methodological tradition Rao Molina (2015) doi:10.1002/9781118735855, computational implementation adapted parameterisation prior-specification conventions ‘brms’ package (Buerkner 2017 doi:10.18637/jss.v080.i01) Stan back-end. package designed support principled Bayesian workflow advocated Gelman et al. (2020) doi:10.48550/arXiv.2011.01808 – prior predictive checks, MCMC convergence diagnostics, posterior predictive checks, leave-one-cross-validation, Bayesian model comparison averaging, prior sensitivity analysis, design-consistent benchmarking part standard pipeline. entry consolidates changelog every development cycle since 0.1.2 maintenance release; package now follows semantic versioning 1.0.0 line constitutes stable user-facing function set whose signatures change v2.0.0.

title: hbsaems 1.0.0
text: Shiny app: memory management multi-model comparison. dashboard’s “snapshot library” lets users compare average predictions across several fitted models one session. snapshot full  object (50-200 MB typical Fay-Herriot fit), session snapshots 5+ models easily blow past 1 GB RAM. Three composable mitigations ship v1.0.0:  multi-model panel now also shows per-snapshot size--MB readout comparison buttons, users can see exactly much memory snapshot costing. Shiny app now exposes Fay-Herriot sampling_variance argument. interactive run_sae_app() previously offered survey-design route fixing ϕi\phi_i Beta-Logitnormal workflow (via n + deff) expose equivalent sampling_variance = "psi_i" slot Lognormal-Lognormal workflow (pins σi=ψi\sigma_i = \sqrt{\psi_i}) Custom (Gaussian / Lognormal / Student) workflow. gaps closed: new “Sampling Variance (psi_i)” dropdown appears Lognormal-Lognormal panel “Sampling Variance (D_i)” dropdown appears Custom panel user picks family supports . Selecting column wires straight hbm_lnln() / hbm(), apply link-override fix release transparently. means classical Fay-Herriot SAE workflow now end--end usable GUI without dropping back scripted calls. Shiny app exposes measurement_error sugar (Ybarra-Lohr 2008). new collapsible “Measurement-error covariates” box Modeling tab lets user mark one auxiliary covariate noisily-measured supply column holding standard error. hbsaems rewrites   transparently. Shiny app exposes generic fixed_params slot. new collapsible “Fixed distributional parameters (advanced)” box Modeling tab accepts R expression evaluating named list (e.g.  ). Power users can now pin distributional parameter (sigma, phi, shape, nu, …) GUI without writing scripts. Shiny app exposes sae_* post-processing helpers. new “Post-processing” sub-tab Results lets user apply  (log / exp),  (centre + std, centre ),  (RSE-threshold cutoff) recent prediction download transformed table. Shiny app exposes multi-model workflows. new “Multi-model” sub-tab Results provides -app library named model snapshots. user clicks “Snapshot current fit” save copy  custom name, selects 2+ snapshots feed  (LOO/WAIC)  (Bayesian model averaging). closes long-standing gap scripted multi-model API GUI workflow. Generic fixed_params mechanism hbm() hbm_flex(). Lets user pin distributional parameter value derived column, scalar, vector, formula evaluated data. Centralises pattern previously coded per-wrapper. hbm_betalogitnorm() refactor. Four operational modes: random ϕ\phi hyperprior; fixed ϕ\phi survey nn deff\mathrm{deff}; user-overridable hyperprior α,β\alpha,\beta via stanvars; generic fixed_params power users. Default priors filled automatically follow Liu (2009). hbm_lnln(sampling_variance = ...) Fay–Herriot lognormal model. Pins σi=ψi\sigma_i = \sqrt{\psi_i} known per-area sampling variance. Custom brms response distributions. Built-support Loglogistic Shifted Loglogistic, plus public extension framework (register_hbsae_brms_custom(), read_stan_function(), build_brms_custom_family()). Stan code lives inst/stan/ plain .stan files. registered family ships log-likelihood, posterior-predict posterior-epred hooks loo(), posterior_predict(), posterior_epred() work box. Spatial random effects. CAR (ICAR / proper / BYM2) SAR (lag / error) via spatial_var, spatial_model, car_type, sar_type, M. Weight matrices can constructed bundled build_spatial_weight() shapefile coordinates. Missing-data handling. Three strategies (deleted, multiple via mice, model via brms::mi()) auto-selection handle_missing = NULL. Shrinkage priors. Horseshoe (regularised, Piironen & Vehtari R2D2 (Zhang et al. 2022) selectable via prior_type. Nonlinear smooth terms full brms-canonical API. Penalised regression splines via  Gaussian processes via : Splines: spline_k (basis dim) spline_bs (basis type: "tp", "cr", "cs", "ps"). GP: gp_k (Hilbert-space approximate GP basis dimension – Riutort-Mayol et al. 2023), gp_cov (covariance function: "exp_quad", "matern15", "matern25", "exponential"), gp_c (boundary-scale factor). Automatic warning exact GP (slow, O(n3)O(n^3)) requested 100 areas, recommended gp_k value. gp_scale deprecated favour gp_c; removal scheduled v2.0.0. Bilingual Shiny dashboard (run_sae_app()). English / Indonesian, dedicated spatial setup tab, -app code preview, CSV / RDS data upload. Source inst/shiny/sae_app/. Benchmarking helpers (sae_benchmark(), sae_predict()). Pfeffermann-style design-consistent benchmarking --sample prediction unsampled areas. Bayesian model averaging LOO weights. model_average() now accepts method argument: "manual" (default, user weights), "stacking", "pseudobma" (via loo::loo_model_weights, canonical Bayesian stacking / pseudo-BMA+ Yao et al. 2018). Power-scale prior sensitivity diagnostics via prior_sensitivity(): thin wrapper around priorsense::powerscale_sensitivity() (Kallioinen et al. 2024) detecting prior-data conflict weak likelihood fitted models. Hierarchical area_var multi-stage SAE. hbm_flex(), hbm_lnln(), hbm_betalogitnorm(), hbm_binlogitnorm() now accept area_var character vector (highest level first, e.g. c("province", "regency")) companion area_re_structure argument selects "nested" (default; produces (1 | province / regency)) "crossed" random intercepts. Length-1 input behaves exactly earlier releases, existing code continues work unchanged. Custom Stan family name prefix hbsae_. avoid symbol collision Stan’s built-loglogistic_lpdf (Stan >= 2.29), Stan function definitions loglogistic shifted loglogistic families now named hbsae_loglogistic_lpdf / hbsae_shifted_loglogistic_lpdf live inst/stan/hbsae_loglogistic.stan / inst/stan/hbsae_shifted_loglogistic.stan. user-facing R helpers (dloglogistic, brms_custom_loglogistic, etc.) registry keys ("loglogistic", "shifted_loglogistic") unchanged. hbm() now accepts sampling_variance = "<col>" Fay-Herriot sugar (previously available hbm_lnln()). Pins  via offset canonical way fit Gaussian Fay-Herriot model. Without residual  area-RE  compete explain variance, producing weak identifiability divergent transitions almost regardless adapt_delta. vignettes using data_fhnorm updated accordingly. Family compatibility check: sampling_variance valid continuous families expose residual SD parameter named sigma (gaussian, lognormal, student, skew_normal, exgaussian, asym_laplace). Passing Beta / Binomial / Poisson / Gamma / Weibull families now raises explicit error pointing user appropriate family-specific mechanism (e.g.fixed_params$phi Beta via design effect, trials Binomial). Centralised sugar--fixed_params translation. previously duplicated translation logic hbm(), hbm_lnln(), hbm_betalogitnorm() now consolidated two internal helpers (, ) consistent validation, conflict checks fixed_params, error messages. Behaviour unchanged user’s point view; refactor eliminates ~60 lines duplicated code. data_fhnorm regenerated deterministic, well-identified simulation (set.seed(20260518L)). Covariates standardised , sigma_u = 1.0,  vignettes fit cleanly default brms / Stan settings. See data-raw/data_fhnorm.R reproducible generator. measurement_error sugar (Ybarra Lohr 2008). hbm() hbm_* wrappers accept  rewrites brmsformula fly wrap listed auxiliary variables . Validation enforces non-negative, NA-free standard errors named variables part auxiliary. Automatic mi() / () detection. user writes   explicitly formula, hbm() longer demands handle_missing set longer drops rows NA: brms’s joint-modelling / measurement-error framework handles imputation internally. Internally handle_missing silently set "model" case. Conditional link_phi resolution hbm_betalogitnorm(). Default now NULL resolves automatically: "identity" phi pinned via fixed_params$phi (survey-design mode), "log" (brms default) phi estimated via hyperprior. eliminates class divergent transitions caused NUTS proposing negative  identity scale. Manually setting "identity" random mode emits warning. Simplified phi prior hbm_betalogitnorm() random mode. pre-existing hierarchical construction  replaced brms’s default  (lower bound 0). wrapper longer declares   Stan parameters longer injects  sampling statements . Rationale:  prior  (declared ) boundary support, producing divergent transitions weakly-informative data; extra layer also inflated effective posterior dimension essentially one scalar parameter per area model.  users relied old construction can build manually via  +  arguments; see  migration note. Legacy code still passes  containing sampling statements  /  now raises informative error pointing user new mechanism. sae_benchmark() defaults made semantically explicit. New argument target_type = c("total", "mean") (default "total") consulted weights = NULL, choosing safe default weighting (rep(1, n) total, rep(1 / n, n) mean). informational message emitted chosen weighting always visible. Production users still pass explicit weights = N_i (population size per area).

what: Naming and interface changes (breaking only at v2.0.0)
title: hbsaems 1.0.0
text: Argument rename: predictors -> auxiliary. new name aligns Small Area Estimation literature (Rao & Molina 2015, Pfeffermann 2013). Old usage continues work one-time soft-deprecation warning scheduled removal v2.0.0. Argument rename: hbm_generic() -> hbm_flex(). Old name removed. Deprecated wrappers (legacy v0.1.x): hbcc(), hbmc(), hbpc(), hbsae(). four emit soft-deprecation warning pointing hbm(), convergence_check(), prior_check(), sae_aggregate(), sae_predict() respectively. Scheduled removal v2.0.0.

title: hbsaems 1.0.0
text: Seven new vignettes covering full workflow: hbsaems-modelling (overview), hbsaems-lnln-model, hbsaems-betalogitnorm-model, hbsaems-binlogitnorm-model, hbsaems-spatial, hbsaems-handle-missing, hbsaems-run_sae_app. follow CRAN-safe pattern: heavy Stan fits evaluated build time; representative outputs printed illustrations explicit disclaimer. Three retained vignettes: complete-workflow, advanced-features, migration-guide. Vignettes updated reflect v1.0.0 changes. hbsaems-betalogitnorm-model vignette now documents two modes ϕ\phi (Random Fixed) instead three, includes legacy-reproduction recipe users need pre-v1.0.0 hierarchical hyperprior. migration-guide vignette gained two new sections covering phi prior simplification critical link function override fix. hbsaems-lnln-model vignette adds implementation note explaining sampling_variance offset now interpreted natural (untransformed) scale.

title: hbsaems 1.0.0
text: CRAN vignette now carries real output. complete-workflow vignette previously used knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval = FALSE) globally showed R code display-. kept CRAN’s vignette build short also meant readers see seven workflow steps actually produces. revised vignette evaluates curated subset chunks: production-grade hbm(..., chains = 4, iter = 4000) calls prior-predictive check, headline model fit, model-comparison, model-averaging kept display- blocks (lowercase, evaluated knitr) – push CRAN vignette build well past time budget. text now explicitly tells readers use full settings analyses, toy iter = 200 demo. Stan-toolchain probe setup chunk silently falls back display-mode Boost / C++ compiler unavailable (e.g. minimal CI container), vignette renders cleanly everywhere – just fewer outputs Stan-less systems. GitHub Actions: automated pkgdown deployment. Three workflows live .github/workflows/:  new  repository root explains first-time setup (GitHub Pages settings, Actions write permissions, URL field DESCRIPTION) local pkgdown::build_site() preview workflow. guide Rbuildignored bloat CRAN tarball. Vignette strategy reorganised CRAN release. v1.0.0 development built extensive collection vignettes covering every distribution-specific wrapper, every advanced topic, internals. Eleven vignettes great documentation set heavy load CRAN tarball; CRAN’s guidance new submissions favours small set essential vignettes. now ship one vignette tarball, complete-workflow, canonical end--end SAE pipeline using hbm() standard Bayesian workflow diagnostics. remaining ten files kept source repository vignettes/articles/ (pkgdown convention) rendered articles package website https://madsyair.github.io/hbsaems/. R CMD build skips vignettes/articles/ via .Rbuildignore, CRAN tarball leaner (-46 KB) without losing documentation – users install CRAN still get website link via URL: field DESCRIPTION. New article: “AST-based Formula Manipulation”. walkthrough hbsaems rewrites user formulas internally apply nonlinear, measurement_error, area_var, sampling_variance, handle_missing sugar – regex-based approach silently corrupt many legitimate formulas. Available https://madsyair.github.io/hbsaems/articles/ast-formula-manipulation.html.

title: hbsaems 1.0.0
text: Test suite reorganisation. Per recommendation external code review, validation-tests (exercising input validation, deprecation warnings, error paths) split heavy integration tests migrated .  block classified static analysis: blocks whose /  call appears inside /  CRAN-safe now run every check; remaining ~60 integration tests actually compile Stan model stay  gated  (). Net effect: CRAN test count rose 391 ~466 measurable runtime increase. Internal helpers    provide brms-compatible shells tests need exercise deprecation pipeline without compiling Stan. Project polish. Added CONTRIBUTING.md documenting development workflow (test tiers, mock-stub usage, AST-based formula manipulation patterns, spelling regeneration); added light .lintr configuration contributors; updated cran-comments.md describe first-submission test environment intentional notes. exported functions documented full roxygen2 blocks including @param, @return, @examples, references appropriate. Custom-distribution Stan code stored separate .stan files inst/stan/ (one source truth, syntax highlighting, string-escaping noise). Test suite reorganised CRAN-safe unit tests (tests/testthat/) heavy integration tests (tests/testthat/dev-tests/, gated skip_on_cran() excluded via .Rbuildignore).

title: hbsaems 0.1.0
text: Initial public release CRAN. Provided hbm(), hbcc(), hbmc(), hbpc(), hbsae(), three example datasets (data_fhnorm, data_lnln, data_betalogitnorm, data_binlogitnorm).
